To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with local councils regarding enforcement against unlicensed Houses in Multiple Occupation, in the last two years.
answer_text: My Department engages with local councils through an established monitoring group, which receives quarterly data on enforcement against unlicensed HMOs. In addition, following a commitment made in my oral statement on 21 October 2025, I wrote to all council Chief Executives to reiterate their statutory responsibilities for HMO licensing and enforcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of unlicensed Houses in Multiple Occupation on tenant safety and housing standards.
answer_text: Consultation and engagement informed the development of the current licensing legislation and it was widely recognised that unregulated HMOs posed increased risks to tenant safety and could lead to poorer housing conditions. Reports of unlicensed HMOs are investigated by local councils, with enforcement action taken where necessary.The Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 and the associated regulations set minimum standards and safety requirements for HMOs. Under Section 14 of the Act, councils can impose additional licensing requirements it deems appropriate, such as minimum energy efficiency standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that Freedom of Information requests are responded to within the statutory timeframe.
answer_text: My Department has an established process for responding to Freedom of Information requests to maximise the number of requests responded to within the statutory timeframe.  The  number of requests has increased by 32% over the last two years, and so my Department continuously reviews processes to ensure it can meet the statutory timeframes.This includes a weekly report to Senior Management highlighting all live requests and the dates they are due. A system of reminders and assistance is provided by my Department’s Information Management team, who also maintain a suite of documentation to help staff provide timely responses.  Regular training is also provided to all staff, including detailing training for staff in receipt of a high volume of requests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether alternative lower-cost translation solutions, such as telephone or digital interpretation services, are being considered within Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department consistently works to explore alternative options to providing all services across the HSC ensuring optimum value for money without compromising the high standard of care expected of the health service.  This includes spoken language interpreting services that are provided for HSC Trusts and Primary care through the Business Services Organisation (BSO).  The provision of this Interpreting Service ensures that my Department and ALBs comply with legislative requirements such as Race Relations (NI) Order 1997 and Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.The vast majority of interpreting is face-to-face, providing not just real time human interaction but often non-verbal cues and addressing cultural differences that can lead to miscommunication.  Face-to-face interpreting also helps build trust between the patient or client particularly in a health and social care setting.  Unfortunately, it is not always possible to provide face-to-face interpreting.  BSO may not have any registered interpreters for the required language, the right interpreter could be booked with another patient, unavailable or have too far to travel.  In these instances, telephone interpreting is available and has been an option for Trusts and GPs etc for more than 20 years.  The contract for telephone interpreting across all Government Departments is held by the Department for Communities.  Primary Care and Trusts are encouraged to use telephone interpreting when appropriate and costs have been shown in AQW 25105 and AQW 27809 amongst others.With advances in technology, the BSO has in recent years been able to offer video call interpreting since 2020 which reduces travel time for interpreters and travel costs.  In the first four years almost 8,000 calls were made this way. Trusts have also piloted hand-held interpreting devices to bridge communication gaps, however, these are currently only suited to certain interactions as explained above.  They do not replace face-to-face interpreting but instead help reduce language barriers for people who do not speak English proficiently.  They support safe, timely and personcentred care during unscheduled care, crisis or emergency situations for individuals but only until a face to face or telephone interpreter can be sourced.  They cannot be used for clinical information.Belfast Trust ran a sixmonth pilot of 33 interpreting devices from May 2025 to November 2025 across a range of unscheduled and emergency care settings, including Emergency Dental, Maternity, and the Emergency Department.  Although the pilot has now concluded, staff across these sites continue to use the devices due to the positive feedback received during evaluation.  Frontline teams reported that the devices provided immediate, practical support at critical moments, such as “urgent care while waiting for a facetoface interpreter” (Maternity).The Trust is planning to purchase an additional 25 devices for other emergency services, including Emergency Social

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration has been given to ensuring equitable access for dialysis patients across the UK.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the rules governing reimbursement for dialysis treatment received abroad by Northern Ireland residents under the UK Global Health Insurance Card differ from those operating in NHS England.
answer_text: The EU-UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA) protocol on social security coordination replicates the previous social security arrangements prior to EU Exit. Under the terms of this international agreement the UK GHIC (Global Health Insurance Card) or UK EHIC (European Health Insurance Card) applies equally to all UK residents and allows access to medically necessary state-provided healthcare in the EU country they are visiting. Medically necessary healthcare means healthcare that cannot reasonably wait until they return to the UK. Any provision, operated by NHS England, for the reimbursement for dialysis treatment outside of the terms of the GHIC/EHIC is a discretionary scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether reimbursement is permitted where dialysis is provided in a private facility due to lack of capacity in a statutory unit.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any arrangements in place for the reimbursement of dialysis treatment received abroad by Northern Ireland residents under the UK Global Health Insurance Card.
answer_text: The EU-UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement replicates the previous social security arrangements prior to EU Exit. A UK GHIC (Global Health Insurance Card) or UK EHIC (European Health Insurance Card) gives UK residents access to medically necessary state-provided healthcare in the EU country they are visiting. Medically necessary healthcare means healthcare that cannot reasonably wait until they return to the UK. Medically necessary healthcare includes things like:•	emergency treatment•	treatment for a long-term or pre-existing medical condition•	routine medical care for pre-existing conditions that need monitoring•	routine maternity care•	oxygen therapy and kidney dialysis.UK residents wishing to access to medically necessary state-provided healthcare when visiting in the EU under the EHIC/GHIC scheme must pre-arrange and obtain agreement in advance from the relevant healthcare provider in the country they plan to visit, for treatments such as, kidney dialysis or chemotherapy, with advice from their own trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to work with (i) local councils; and (ii) property owners, to bring long-term vacant premises in town centres back into use.
answer_text: My officials engage regularly with local Councils to develop initiatives to bring long-term vacant premises in town centres back in to use. Successful schemes have been funded in Belfast and Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council areas which have allowed property owners to avail of grants to redevelop empty units and enable them to be occupied. I have also recently announced the North West Regeneration Fund which will regenerate properties within the Historic Walled City Conservation area of Londonderry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department maintains an up-to-date audit of vacant commercial properties in town centres.
answer_text: My Department does not audit vacant commercial properties in town centres.  Land & Property Services (LPS) collect, process and manage land and property information.  Aggregate statistical data on non-domestic properties, including vacancy rates, is provided on a regular basis by LPS to the Department for Communities for inclusion in the Town Centre Database.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any criteria used by her Department to prioritise the installation of dropped kerbs.
answer_text: Blue Badge Holders, who have had an application for an accessible parking bay outside or near to their home approved, can request a dropped kerb.  The request will be considered where the applicant is dependent upon the use of a wheelchair and there are no other dropped kerbs in the vicinity or, exceptionally, where an applicant, who is not dependent on the use of a wheelchair, can provide evidence of severe mobility difficulties. Beyond this, dropped kerbs or graded pedestrian accesses will generally only be provided in response to requests from individuals after an assessment of the level of provision in the general vicinity has been undertaken and where it is considered that a number of people would benefit from such provision. More comprehensive details of my Department’s approach to providing dropped kerbs can be found by accessing our ‘Provision of Dropped Kerb’ policy document via the following link Provision of dropped kerbs RSPPG E043 | Department for Infrastructure and the application form can be accessed via the following link : Article 80 - Application for Vehicular Access - July 2024.My Department does not hold records of the information requested in a readily accessible format and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of whether current budget allocation processes adequately reflect the high proportion of fixed staffing costs within certain Executive Departments.
answer_text: Public sector workers deserve to be appropriately rewarded for the vital role they play in delivering public services. I have already been clear that, for future Budgets, each Minister should ensure that pay awards are factored into budget plans from the start of the financial year. I recognise that public sector pay is a major component of departments’ expenditure.It is for therefore for each Minister to determine how their budget allocation is used in terms of making sure that staff are rewarded fairly, while ensuring there is enough funding for the other aspects of public service delivery.Proper workforce planning will be essential to departments achieving this, including working alongside their arms-length bodies as appropriate. Transformation and reform may also play an important role.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any progress that has been made on advancing public service reform across Departments.
answer_text: All members of the Executive recognise the urgent need to reform public services to meet the growing needs of our population. We know all too well the pressures in public services as demand continues to rise within a challenging financial climate. We have taken some significant steps in the last two years. These include: within TEO, establishing a Delivery Unit with an initial clear focus on driving down waiting times. Establishing an Office of Science and Technology bringing together departments, industry and world class academic institutions to tackle complex issues that no single organisation can solve alone. Establishing an Office of AI and Digital which is leading the development of a forwardlooking ethical AI Strategy. Within The Executive Office 300 staff have undertaken AI training and this is a key area of focus for training across the service. The £235m Public Sector Transformation Fund has committed £130m in driving service level reform in Healthcare, Special Education Needs, Speeding up Justice and addressing constraints in our waste water infrastructure. The 2nd phase will launch the strategy. Whilst we consider the content of the draft Investment Strategy, a key part of this is the Enabling Action Plan developed by the NICS Board’s ISNI Committee. This includes 12 actions targeting improvements in infrastructure delivery across the themes of People, Process and Policy. We know that people are at the heart of reform and the Department of Finance continues to lead a comprehensive, NICS-wide programme of workforce and organisational reform through the 5-year NICS People Strategy (2025–2030). There is a shared recognition of the urgent need to modernise systems and improve outcomes for citizens. These reforms represent a move towards a more coherent, data-driven and future-focused public service capable of meeting rising demands within a challenging financial context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any actions being taken by her Department, to implement the use of permanent 20mph speed limits outside of schools in predominantly residential areas and town centres.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3. This Tranche has predominantly focused on schools in rural areas where the national speed limit would normally apply.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also taking forward a review on a number of speed related matters, including 20mph speed limits which is open for consultation.  Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation. I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to increase the number of funded nursery places, to reduce competition between families.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for managing the pre-school admissions process and ensuring sufficient places are available to accommodate all target age children whose parents want a place. My Department plays no role in this process. The EA has advised that the total number of funded pre-school education places currently available for the 2026/27 academic year is 21,750, with 19,028 first preference applications submitted in Stage One of the 2026/27 pre-school admissions process. This would indicate that sufficient funded pre-school education places are currently available to meet demand. In 2025/26, 89% of children received an offer of a funded place in their parent’s first preference setting. In each of the last twelve years, at least 99.8% of children whose parents stayed with the process to the end received an offer of a funded pre-school education place in a setting of their parent’s preference. It is, however, not possible, nor is it the aim of the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP), to accommodate every child in their parents’ first preference setting. The EA will continually review pre-school provision levels in all areas and where additional funded places are required, may adjust allocations to settings accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any evidence used to justify the current priority given to those who fall under Socially Disadvantaged Circumstances criterion, in nursery admissions.
answer_text: The Socially Disadvantaged Circumstances (SDC) criterion was established in legislation to prioritise access to funded pre-school places for those children considered most likely to benefit from that provision, as pre-school education provision at the time was limited. Children of both working and non-working parents can be prioritised under the current SDC criterion.My Department does not hold data regarding which admission criterion children are admitted to pre-schools – this is a matter for the management of individual settings, which will apply their own admissions criteria in the event the setting received more applications than it has funded places available. The employment status of parents is not collected during the pre-school admissions process; however, in each of the last five years, at least 99.8% of all children whose parents stayed with the admission process to the end received the offer of a funded place in a preferred setting by the end of stage two of the process. In each of those years funded places also remained available at the end of the process for anyone who had not yet accessed a place for any reason. I am of the view that the legislative SDC priority criterion is no longer necessary or fit for purpose in this context of universal pre-school provision. My Department carried out a public consultation in relation to this in 2021, the details of which are available on the Department’s website. It was clear from the consultation that overall, there is no longer support for the indefinite retention of a statutory priority criterion, however some stakeholders strongly asserted that any change should only be made in the context of the standardisation of pre-school session length. It is my intention therefore to revisit this issue in the context of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, the draft of which was published for public consultation on 17th December 2025 and includes a proposed action to consult on legislative changes to remove the current statutory admissions criteria. I would encourage anyone who has not yet done so to respond to the public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the proportion of applicants who were refused a nursery place, who were from working households, not in receipt of qualifying benefits, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Socially Disadvantaged Circumstances (SDC) criterion was established in legislation to prioritise access to funded pre-school places for those children considered most likely to benefit from that provision, as pre-school education provision at the time was limited. Children of both working and non-working parents can be prioritised under the current SDC criterion.My Department does not hold data regarding which admission criterion children are admitted to pre-schools – this is a matter for the management of individual settings, which will apply their own admissions criteria in the event the setting received more applications than it has funded places available. The employment status of parents is not collected during the pre-school admissions process; however, in each of the last five years, at least 99.8% of all children whose parents stayed with the admission process to the end received the offer of a funded place in a preferred setting by the end of stage two of the process. In each of those years funded places also remained available at the end of the process for anyone who had not yet accessed a place for any reason. I am of the view that the legislative SDC priority criterion is no longer necessary or fit for purpose in this context of universal pre-school provision. My Department carried out a public consultation in relation to this in 2021, the details of which are available on the Department’s website. It was clear from the consultation that overall, there is no longer support for the indefinite retention of a statutory priority criterion, however some stakeholders strongly asserted that any change should only be made in the context of the standardisation of pre-school session length. It is my intention therefore to revisit this issue in the context of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, the draft of which was published for public consultation on 17th December 2025 and includes a proposed action to consult on legislative changes to remove the current statutory admissions criteria. I would encourage anyone who has not yet done so to respond to the public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of convictions for (i) drink; and (ii) drug, driving related offences, in 2025.
answer_text: In 2025, there were 2,216 convictions in cases where there was a prosecution for a drink or drug driving offence.  From information held, it is not always possible to distinguish whether a driving under the influence offence related specifically to drink or drugs, as offence descriptions do not specify the substance involved, so it is not possible to provide information split into the categories listed in the question.Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was a conviction for at least one drink or drugs driving related offence.3. Figures for 2025 are based on management information and should be considered provisional. Finalised prosecutions and convictions statistics for 2025 should be available in early summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any actions currently being considered by her Department to reduce the level of drug-related harm and offending.
answer_text: I recognise the significant harm that drug misuse can cause to individuals, families and communities, and I remain committed to supporting a balanced and partnership-based approach to reducing drug-related harm and offending.My department continues to engage closely with the Department of Health on the implementation of the Substance Use Strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, which is led by the Department of Health and coproduced across government, statutory agencies and the community and voluntary sector. This engagement ensures that justice perspectives are embedded in the ongoing development and delivery of actions to reduce harm associated with drug misuse.In parallel, my department works closely with law enforcement partners through the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) to support efforts to tackle drugs and reduce the harm they cause in our communities. The OCTF is overseen by a Strategy Group, with senior representation from statutory and law enforcement partners, and includes a number of thematic subgroups, one of which focuses specifically on drugs. The OCTF Drugs Subgroup, chaired by the PSNI, brings together relevant agencies on a strategic basis to ensure awareness of emerging threats and to coordinate activity. The subgroup meets quarterly and provides oversight of enforcement action to target those involved in the illicit supply and distribution of drugs and to reduce the harm and offending associated with organised criminality.At a local level, Policing and Community Safety Partnerships [PCSPs], funded by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in responding to community safety concerns. Through local action planning and partnership working with councils, the PSNI and community organisations, PCSPs support initiatives to address drug and alcohol misuse and improve community confidence in policing.I will continue to work with partners across justice, health and the community sector to ensure actions to reduce drugrelated harm and offending remain responsive and under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the case For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers, to outline how the protection of women's safe spaces is being implemented by his Department and its arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: I am clear in my view of the need to protect the integrity of single-sex spaces, and I welcome the judgment from the Supreme Court. In the absence of statutory guidance on this important issue from the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, my officials are looking at the legislative basis for providing the proper clarity and guidance to my Department’s arm’s-length bodies.I am seeking further legal advice on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether people with epilepsy are required to prove a driving licence rejection, before qualifying for the SmartPass scheme.
answer_text: To be eligible for a SmartPass under the category of ‘Driving License Refused’ an individual must have had a driving license refused or revoked on medical grounds for at least one year and they must still be unable to drive. The application for a SmartPass under this category must be sent to an officer in the Driver and Vehicle Agency to be signed and stamped as proof of driving license refusal/revocation. This process is the same regardless of the underlying medical condition.Where an individual has not previously applied for a drivers license but feels their condition would prevent them from driving they should make an application for a provisional license to DVA and indicate their medical condition. DVA will then send them a Medical Declaration Form which they should complete and return. After considering the Medical Declaration, DVA will notify the individual of their decision. If the individual is assessed as being unfit to drive for a period of at least 1 year they can then complete the Smartpass application and forward to DVA for authorisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department has taken to remove the barriers for people with epilepsy, when applying for access to Translink's SmartPass scheme.
answer_text: In 2023, the Department commenced a review of the  Concessionary Fares Scheme.  The Consultation ran from 1st June 2023 to 24th August 2023 and included a number of options which would make the Scheme more accessible for disabled people.During the consultation, some stakeholders also took the opportunity to raise wider issues with the Department, including concerns regarding the SmartPass application process which requires that people with epilepsy, who cannot drive, must first apply for a driving licence and provide evidence of refusal, to be eligible for the concession. Officials are continuing to consider the wider issues raised during the public consultation, including how to make the Scheme more accessible for those already entitled to apply.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of epilepsy specialist nurses in hospitals, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of adult epilepsy specialist nurses working in hospitals over the past five years is as follows:• 2021: 8• 2022: 8• 2023: 8• 2024: 10• 2025: 11It is important to note that not all epilepsy specialist nurses are based in hospitals. In addition to the figures above, there are currently five epilepsy specialist nurses working within community settings.There have also been eight paediatric epilepsy specialist nurses in post across both hospital and community services in each of the last five years.Despite ongoing financial pressures during this period, dedicated recurrent funding has been secured to support three Specialist Neurology Nurse posts. This investment strengthens efforts to reduce waiting times and improve patient care.The Regional Neurology Review highlights that addressing service gaps and regional inequalities will require an increase in specialist nurses across all Trusts. It also notes that delivering a modern, personcentred, and wellresourced neurology service is dependent on a significant expansion of specialist nurse capacity.While I acknowledge that access to epilepsy specialist nurses in Northern Ireland remains limited, the Review also makes clear that full implementation will require substantial additional funding. This funding has not yet been secured and will be subject to future budget allocations. As a result, the availability of funding will directly influence both the scale and pace of implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any ongoing supply disruptions of epilepsy medication.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons. The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department and other operators within the supply chain. My Department is aware that there have been some UK-wide supply disruptions affecting various strengths and preparations of medications for epilepsy in recent times, including carbamazepine, phenytoin, topirmate and lamotrigine products. Although most of these disruptions appear to have now been resolved, there are some strengths and preparations experiencing ongoing disruptions. Communications are issued to healthcare professionals providing them with advice and guidance on the actions to take if supply disruptions do occur. These communications include National Patient Safety Alerts, Medicine Supply Notifications or information provided on the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Services (SPS) medicines supply tool. The SPS medicines supply tool is updated regularly by DHSC and provides up-to-date information on any ongoing supply disruptions, including alternative treatments and anticipated resupply dates. If anyone is experiencing an ongoing supply disruption with their medication for epilepsy, they should discuss this with their prescriber or a healthcare professional who can provide them with appropriate advice on their treatment options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will give any consideration to the expansion of epilepsy services.
answer_text: The Regional Neurology Review acknowledges that current supply of epilepsy specialist nurses is below what is required for the population of Northern Ireland.The Review recognises that addressing service gaps and regional inequalities will require more specialist nurses across all Trusts, and that achieving a modern, personcentred, wellresourced neurology service depends on significantly increasing specialist nurse capacity.I acknowledge that access to epilepsy specialist nurses in Northern Ireland remains limited. However, it must be acknowledged that the Review outlines that full implementation will require substantial additional funding, which has not yet been secured and will depend on future budget allocations. Therefore, identification of funding will have an impact on the scale and pace of implementation.I also acknowledge the important role that data plays in strengthening health intelligence, and I recognise the potential value of an epilepsy register in enhancing epilepsy services. However, with all HSC Trusts now operating on encompass, this unified digital care record could offer a strong foundation from which conditionspecific registers, such as an epilepsy register, can be developed in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's actions in combating bovine TB levels in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In January, on the recommendation of the Chief Vet, I was pleased to establish a new stakeholder body - the TB Partnership Steering Group - to help us address the challenges of getting bovine TB under control in Northern Ireland.  This work led to the Group’s Blueprint for Eradication, which I endorsed and was published in April. New departmental structures are now in place to oversee the implementation of the Blueprint with initial work focused on increasing the use of interferon gamma testing; the provision of on farm biosecurity advice; improved TB testing; preparing a new consultation on potential wildlife intervention options; and an innovative new regionalisation initiative in partnership with the Republic.Last month, the Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and the Marine, Martin Heydon TD, and I announced this new initiative which will take a regionalised approach to tackling bovine TB in a defined border area, focusing on coordinated measures to reduce incidence and transmission.  This pilot has now been awarded funding of €6.4 million in the Irish Government’s announcement on the Shared Island programme on 17 November. My officials are now working at pace with counterparts in the Department of Agriculture, Fisheries and the Marine to finalise details of the regionalisation pilot which is anticipated to begin next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any information her Department holds on the cross-border illegal immigration offences detected and prosecuted in the last 12 months.
answer_text: ANSWER – AQO 2789/22-27In Northern Ireland, immigration and asylum policy including illegal migration is not devolved. It remains the responsibility of the UK Home Office, just as in the rest of the United Kingdom. The Department holds information on offences prosecuted at courts here, and in relation to any subsequent convictions for offences relating to illegal entry to the United Kingdom as part of its prosecutions and convictions datasets.  Such offences are prosecuted under the Immigration Act 1971 and the Nationality and Borders Act 2022.  However, the information held does not detail whether the offences related to instances of illegal entry to Northern Ireland via the land border from the Republic of Ireland.In 2024, the most recent year for which official statistics are available, there were 66 cases prosecuted at courts by PPS on behalf of police and other Governmental bodies, excluding appeals, resulting in 63 convictions.  In 38 of these cases a custodial disposal was imposed, a suspended sentence in 22 of the cases and, in 3 of the cases, a monetary penalty was the main disposal imposed. That said, as Justice Minister I am of course conscious of the unique challenges posed in Northern Ireland around these issues and will continue to work with law enforcement partners, including on a cross-border basis, to tackle the matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any investigations conducted to understand and address the risk of sexual assaults within hospitals, since he took up office.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he has taken to reduce the risk of sexual assaults within hospitals, since he took up office.
answer_text: HSC Trusts were approached for information on any actions taken to reduce the risk of sexual assaults within hospitals since May 2024 until early December 2025.  The following information was received.South Eastern HSC TrustThe SEHSCT continues to implement the regional Management of Violence and Aggression (MOVA) framework in an effort to support staff and reduce all incidents of Violence and Aggression against staff.  This includes encouraging staff and managers to risk assess, attend relevant training and report incidents as they arise.Southern HSC TrustThe SHSCT has long standing policies and guidance on the management of incidents.Line managers are prompted, while undertaking the risk assessment process, to consider sexual assault and to put in suitable measures to mitigate such a hazard.Northern HSC TrustIn the NHSCT any assaults on staff, are covered by the Regional Management of Violence and Aggression Policy (Violence and Aggression in the Workplace – Regional Framework - NHSCT/24/2028) and the work the Trust undertakes in that respect, although sexual assaults are not considered specifically. Sexual assaults have been identified as a priority in our HWI action plan to be completed during 2026.Western HSC TrustThe WHSCT routinely records and reviews lessons learned when reporting incidents, which includes a range of measures that staff can undertake to reduce the risk of such incidents.  This information is used to inform changes to local policies, improve staff training, strengthen clinical and security practices and support wider organisation learning. The Trust is also piloting the introduction of body worn cameras, with evidence suggesting this may support the de-escalation of incidents.’BHSCT ResponseNo HROD response

Further information on the Roads 2025-26 programme of works are currently being published in annual council reports which can be found at the link below: www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/topics/council-reports

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his engagement with businesses who applied to the Small Business Research Initiative aimed at tackling the growth of blue-green algae in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: My Department recently announced the allocation of £2 million in funding to four suppliers who have progressed to Phase 2 of the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI). This initiative is aimed at mitigating and treating Blue-Green Algae in Lough Neagh and other affected waterbodies. The outputs of Phase 1 have been encouraging. However, continued research and development is essential to validate these models at scale and to assess their commercial viability.Phase 2, which launched on Monday 18 August, builds upon the work undertaken during Phase 1 by the five suppliers selected through open competition. It aims to further develop the proof-of-concept models established in the initial phase.My Department also continues to lead on other SBRI projects to contribute to nutrient management, including the ‘Sustainable Use of Livestock Slurry’ and ‘Sustainable Use of Layer Manure.’ In addition, my Department is working collaboratively with UK Space on a ‘Remote Sensing’ SBRI which when combined with DAERA ground truthing, will enhance our understanding of how blue green algae blooms, and spreads within the Lough.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has made an assessment of the potential impact of asylum accommodation contracts on the private rented housing market.
answer_text: In September I met with the Regional Head of Operations and Chief Strategy Officer of Mears. During that meeting I expressed my concerns regarding the impacts their contracts are having on the local housing market. My Department has not undertaken a formal assessment however, all available evidence indicates that the private rented sector is already under significant pressure, therefore any additional demand will further exacerbate existing supply challenges.

To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the development of proposals, which will ensure that social landlords have appropriate powers to deal with anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: I recently brought forward a paper to the Executive in relation to legislative proposals for inclusion in a Housing Bill. Specifically, this covered injunctions against unacceptable behaviour, grounds for possession and amendments to eligibility for social housing and Full Duty homelessness status where unacceptable behaviour has occurred. 		The paper was agreed by the Executive on 02 October 2025 and my Department has now commenced formal engagement with the Office of the Legislative Counsel to finalise the policy and timetabling of the Bill. 	It is anticipated that the Bill will be introduced in early 2026, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) how she will address road safety concerns on the Dungannon Road, Portadown; and (ii) when a road safety assessment will be carried out at this location.
answer_text: Road safety is an extremely high priority for my Department and it is committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. Officials will review this stretch of road in relation to road safety to ensure that that the existing signage and road markings are appropriate and comply with the Department’s current policy guidelines.Officials have advised that the current speed limit of 40mph on the Dungannon Road from Corcrain to Drumcree Road is considered appropriate and in line with my Department’s policy.The National Speed Limit which applies from the Drumcree Road to The Birches Roundabout is also considered appropriate. Officials are also currently in the process of reviewing the latest Road Traffic Collision data from the PSNI for accident cluster sites. If Dungannon Road is identified as a site and a common causation factor can be identified, then effective road safety measures may be introduced, if appropriate. I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year.Concerns regarding driver behaviour or speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how he intends to strengthen microchipping requirements for dogs.
answer_text: I remain committed to bolstering microchipping requirements in Northern Ireland and my officials are currently considering a range of options. Any proposals will be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any additional speed signage that can be installed on the Dungannon Road, Portadown, to improve road safety.
answer_text: Road safety is an extremely high priority for my Department and it is committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. Officials have advised that the current speed limit of 40mph and the associated signage on the Dungannon Road from Corcrain to Drumcree Road is considered appropriate and in line with my Department’s policy.The National Speed Limit which applies from the Drumcree Road to The Birches Roundabout is also considered appropriate. My Department’s current policy is that we do not provide repeater signs for roads where the National Speed Limit applies as this would lead to an over proliferation of unnecessary signage. To do so in some cases but not others would result in inconsistency, driver uncertainty and enforcement issues.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling. This important work is ongoing and will be subject to public consultation later this year.Concerns regarding driver behaviour should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Consultation on the Introduction of Mandatory CCTV into Slaughterhouses.
answer_text: I am pleased that the public consultation, on proposals to make CCTV mandatory in slaughterhouses, was favourably received. The Consultation Response and Way Forward document was recently published on my Department’s website and work is underway to draft the legislation required to implement the proposal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on (i) the implementation of part-time 20mph speed limit zones outside schools in Upper Bann; and (ii) when future tranches will be announced.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website, link; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking and cycling.My Department is also carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to prepare for adverse winter weather conditions in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon local council area.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements have been finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and salt stocks have been replenished. My Department commits significant resources to its winter service programme and every night, from the end of October, up to 300 people will be on standby ready to salt main roads, helping drivers across the north to cope with the wintry conditions. Contracts are also in place to enable contractors and farmers to help to clear roads during periods of prolonged snow. While my Department targets the limited resources available for winter service on the busier main through routes, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my department, which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Our salt reserves have been filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of winter service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required.In addition, my Department has an arrangement with Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon District Council to salt city and town centre footways during times of prolonged ice and snow, and the council will be contacted to ensure continuity of this service for the incoming winter season should conditions dictate.My Department’s Winter Service Policy can be viewed via the following link : RSPPG E022, and details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via DFI Roads Salted NetworkOur routine maintenance work to provide an annual clean to all DfI gullies in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon District Council area is also on-going alongside the routine cleaning of roadside channels and open drains and will continue throughout the winter season.In preparation for and during flood events local staff will utilise available resources to support community resilience groups in flood risk areas and respond, where possible, to requests for assistance from the public where property has suffered or is threatened by flooding. Departmental depots are fully stocked with sandbags for temporary flood protection which will be distributed directly when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the safety of probation staff when working with high-risk offenders.
answer_text: The Probation Board for Northern Ireland takes its safety obligations towards all staff seriously.  A number of measures are in place to ensure the safety of staff working with high-risk offenders. These include physical measures and security infrastructure in probation offices; appropriate training on personal safety and other robust processes and procedures; and establishing clear expectations with Service Users on what is acceptable behaviour.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any discussions he has had with the Mears Group, in the last two years.
answer_text: I met with Mears on 10 September 2025 to discuss and understand the scope of their activity locally.  I took this opportunity to raise concerns regarding their dispersal accommodation, the impacts on the private rental sector and challenges with community cohesion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the process of investigating reports of unlicensed houses in multiple occupation.
answer_text: The powers to investigate reports of unlicensed houses in multiple occupation (HMOs) sits with local council and the NIHMO Unit based in Belfast City Council. The NIHMO unit also has responsibility for the administration of the licensing scheme on behalf of all councils, including ensuring enforcement action is taken against those operating unlicensed HMOs. I have been assured that all reports of unlicensed HMOs are investigated.I have written to all council Chief Executives to remind them of council responsibilities for HMOs and the extensive powers under the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 to investigate, enforce and punish those landlords acting outside the law.  My letter also advised their Environmental Health Officers remain vigilant to the potential for properties to be operating as unlicensed HMOs and for the prompt reporting of any concerns to the NIHMO Unit.

answer_text: This information has been provided by the NIHMO Unit based in Belfast City Council. Due to the volume of information, it has been placed in the Assembly Library.

answer_text: There are currently no dogs living permanently within Maghaberry Prison.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) number; and (ii) value, of successful applications to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, in the Upper Bann constituency.
answer_text: As of 10 September 2025, a total of 1,501 children in the Upper Bann constituency were registered for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS), and £1,068,118 was claimed by childcare providers through the scheme to reduce childcare costs for working parents.AQW 31146/22-27, which was published in the weekly answers booklet and deposited in the Assembly Library for week ending 19 September 2025 provided a breakdown across all constituencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on the accessibility of NHS-funded hybrid closed loop technology for those with type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  I have recently supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout. The implementation plan is currently being drafted and will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether Northern Ireland has formally committed to relocating a set number of Afghan refugees under the (i) Afghan Relocations and Assistance Policy; and (ii) the Afghan Citizens Resettlement Scheme.
answer_text: It was agreed in September 2021, in line with our population proportion, that we would receive 3% of those Afghan citizens being relocated and resettled in the UK.  This equated to 840 persons in total, with initial estimates of 240 arrivals under the Afghan Relocations and Assistance Policy (ARAP) in Year 1, and 120 arrivals per year, for 5 years, under Afghan Citizens Resettlement Scheme (ACRS). However, arrival numbers to date have been significantly lower than this.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount of funding that has been offered by the UK Government for managing and distributing support through the (i) Afghan Relocations and Assistance Policy; and (b) the Afghan citizens resettlement scheme.
answer_text: Home Office has provided ringfenced funding to support the resettlement of Afghans under both the Afghan Citizens Resettlement Scheme (ACRS) and the Afghan Relocations and Assistance Policy (ARAP) since these schemes were introduced. On 1 March 2025, Westminster brought together the existing Afghan resettlement and relocation schemes, including ACRS and ARAP, into a single Afghan Resettlement Programme (ARP). Home Office amended the funding instructions at the same time, and since 1 March 2025 the funding provided by Westminster, per arrival, under the ARP consists of: • Year 1 Integration Tariff: £12,410 per arrival • Year 1 Education Tariff: £2,965 per child (age 3-4) or £5,130 per child (age 5- 18) • Year 1 Health Tariff: £2,600 per arrival • Year 2–3 Funding (intended as a contribution towards costs incurred supporting an individual’s continued participation in the Schemes): Year 2 = £6,550 per arrival and Year 3 = £5,150 per arrival. At present, arrivals here will have spent time in GB within transitional accommodation before moving. Therefore, a proportion of the Year 1 tariffs (integration, education and health) may be claimed by the local authority in GB who has initially supported the individual / family. The quantum of Year 1 tariff that NI Departments receive is dependent on the length of time arrivals have initially spent in GB.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any steps which have been taken to prioritise veterans presenting at local authorities as homeless for housing assistance.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for providing homelessness assistance in Northern Ireland. When exercising its functions in relation to housing allocation, homelessness and disabled facilities grants, due regard must be given to the principle of the Armed Forces Covenant that service members, veterans, and their families are treated fairly and receive the support that they need, including access to social housing in Northern Ireland.When an individual presents as homeless, the Housing Executive will carry out a Homelessness Assessment based on four legislative tests: homelessness, eligibility, priority need and intentionality, in accordance with Article 5 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1988.I continue to engage and recently met with the NI Veterans Commissioner’s Office to discuss matters regarding veteran housing and the Armed Forces Covenant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the effectiveness of abattoirs in enforcing welfare standards during the production of halal meat.
answer_text: DAERA employed Official Veterinarians deliver Official Controls in all abattoirs in Northern Ireland. This includes the production of halal meat. Part of the duties required, includes the enforcement of animal welfare legislation. I am confident that welfare standards are maintained effectively.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any recent assessment he has made of the reasons for the attainment gap between boys and girls at the end of Key Stage 2.
answer_text: My reintroduction of end of Key Stage Assessment in 2024-25, alongside the funding and promotion of participation of pupils across our schools in International Large-Scale Assessments has demonstrated commitment to ensuring that my Department has access to a robust, time series of indicators of educational attainment. This will contribute to providing a more comprehensive evidence base to enable greater analysis of trends and impact, and ability to more effectively identify and address the reasons for any attainment gap.The CCEA Summary Report on Interim End of Key Stage Statutory Assessment in 2024-25 published on 26 August 2025 highlighted a larger proportion of female pupils were at or above level 4 (74.8%) than male pupils (69.2%) in Communication. However, for using Mathematics a similar proportion of female (71.6%) and male (72.5%) pupils were at or above level 4.This gender attainment gap in Communication aligns with results from the Progress in International Reading Literacy Study (PIRLS 2011, 2016, 2021) and previous end of Key Stage assessments. Both of these sources demonstrate that girls have continuously performed better than boys and this gender gap favouring girls has increased over time. The reason for the increasing gender gap in Northern Ireland is increasing attainment for girls compared to a more stable pattern for boys.PIRLS 2021 also showed that the persistent gender gap in reading is not unique to Northern Ireland but is a global educational challenge. Research indicates a wide variety of reasons for this including biological cognitive and development differences, societal and attitudinal pressures and a higher enjoyment of reading in girls.This highlights the urgent need for a renewed focus on literacy across our schools and that is why I will be publishing new literacy guidance for schools supported by a targeted programme of teacher professional learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any steps she is taking to support retail workers in reporting incidents of (i) abuse; (ii) threats; and (iii) violence, to the PSNI.
answer_text: Business and retail crime has a real impact on local businesses, staff, individual consumers and the wider community, and a partnership approach is central to preventing and addressing retail crime. My Department is fully committed to working with all partners, including businesses, the community and voluntary sector, the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), and other Departments and organisations to ensure that we have a community where we all feel safe to live and work.  My Department is a member of the Business Crime Partnership (BCP).  The BCP, chaired by the PSNI, consists of representatives from the Department of Justice, Northern Ireland Policing Board, Northern Ireland Retail Consortium, Federation of Small Businesses, Retail NI, Belfast City Centre Management, Trading Standards and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs).  The BCP is a networking, information sharing and engagement forum, set up to share best practice and crime prevention advice to support businesses.  Additionally, my Department, alongside the Northern Ireland Policing Board provides £4.25m annual operational funding to PCSPs to identify and help address local community safety issues, including the issue of business and retail crime. PCSPs address the issue of business crime via a number of initiatives including on-street projects such as Community Safety Warden schemes, radio link and text alert schemes as well as the Business Watch scheme which provides crime prevention advice. BCP members work together to encourage retailers to report crime to the PSNI and reassure them that no incident is too small to report and it is helpful to note that PSNI recently launched their Virtual Officer Attendance Service which allows officers to take statements and collect digital evidence virtually which provides flexibility and convenience for retailers and others when reporting crime. You may be aware that the assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm; however, in light of increasing concerns about assaults on a range of workers who serve the public, including retail workers, I also intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing a public service, performing a public duty, or providing services to the public.  The new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that currently available for the offence of common assault, which applies regardless of the victim’s occupation.  Alongside this new offence, I intend to make assault on such a person in more serious cases a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect that fact in their sentencing decisions.  I intend to introduce this Sentencing Bill before the end of 2025, subject to Executive approval.   The direct response to any criminal activity is primarily an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policin

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average length of time between charge and trial in cases of sexual abuse which have yet to heard in court.
answer_text: Information for the period from charge/summons to first court date after a decision by Public Prosecution Service (PPS) to prosecute a person had been taken has been provided, as not all cases result in a trial and a significant proportion are dealt with by way of a guilty plea.  At 1 September 2025, for cases where a decision by PPS to prosecute a person had been taken but where the first court date after that decision had not yet been reached, the average (median) time since the defendant had been charged or summonsed, in cases where the main offence was a sexual offence was 6 months 11 days.  Note:1. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, NCA, Harbour, Airport police and other Governmental bodies in Northern Ireland.2. Figures include cases at Crown Court and at magistrates’ courts.3. Figures relate to cases where the main offence is a sexual offence.4. The time measured is up to the first court date after PPS decision to prosecute.  5. Figures are derived from management information systems.6. The form of average used is the median, as this removes any skew caused by cases where particular circumstances may have caused them to remain incomplete for a longer than normal period of time.7. The date of first court date after PPS decision to prosecute used in this calculation is not necessarily the same as the first date of trial. The Department of Justice is working with criminal justice partners to deliver a Speeding Up Justice programme, which seeks to reform the handling of cases, getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, and dealing with cases effectively and proportionately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the longest projected length of time between charge and trial in cases of sexual abuse yet to be heard in court.
answer_text: Information for the period from charge/summons to first court date after a decision by Public Prosecution Service (PPS) to prosecute a person had been taken has been provided, as not all cases result in a trial and a significant proportion are dealt with by way of a guilty plea.  At 1 September 2025, for cases where the main offence was a sexual offence and where a decision by Public Prosecution Service to prosecute a person had been taken but where the first court date after that decision had not yet been reached, in the case where the longest time had passed since the defendant had been charged or summonsed, it had been 14 years 4 months.  Note:1. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, NCA, Harbour, Airport police and other Governmental bodies in Northern Ireland.2. Figures include cases at Crown Court and at magistrates’ courts.3. Figures relate to cases where the main offence is a sexual offence.4. The time measured is up to the first court date after PPS decision to prosecute.  5. Figures are derived from management information systems.6. The date of first court date after PPS decision to prosecute used in this calculation is not necessarily the same as the first date of trial.There may be a number of reasons that a case may have been unable to be completed, such as where the defendant has health related issues, where a person has absconded, or where a defendant is already in custody in another jurisdiction.  The Department of Justice is working with criminal justice partners to deliver a Speeding Up Justice programme, which seeks to reform the handling of cases, getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, and dealing with cases effectively and proportionately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any further developments in relation to Northern Ireland's participation in the America 250 celebrations.
answer_text: The Department has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the America250 Commission confirming the special status secured for Northern Ireland in the 250th anniversary commemorations and recognising the important contribution made by men and women from Ulster to the establishment of the United States. I have invited the Commission to visit Northern Ireland during the autumn so they can see firsthand the strength of these connections.Department officials are finalising a scheme to disburse the £250k funding I announced for US250 related events, and I can confirm this will be ready to launch before the end of the year.The Department, its Arm’s Length Bodies and Strategic Partners are developing a programme of events to commemorate the American 250th anniversary in 2026 as part of the US-NI Cultural Working Group. The programme will include: exhibitions, documentaries, publications, music, trails and new visitor experiences. I will announce more details later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the businesses she engaged with during her visit to New York in March 2025, undertaken alongside Invest NI.
answer_text: In March 2025, I travelled to both New York and Boston with Invest NI for a series of business networking engagements and one-to-one meetings with potential inward investors, business partners, and stakeholders. Prospective investor meetings are held on a confidential basis to protect commercially sensitive information and respect the privacy of all parties.In Boston, I met with existing investors Rapid7 and Globalization Partners, as well as members of the US Net Zero Advisory Board. I addressed a business leaders’ lunch attended by over 70 guests, reflecting on the strong and successful collaboration between the US and the North.In New York, I met with Factor Law and hosted a business lunch with over 80 attendees, where I highlighted the benefits of dual market access for investors.I also met and welcomed the international success of Belfast-based Artemis Technologies, who signed a multimillion-dollar deal to advance clean maritime solutions in the US.The visit strengthened relationships with influential business, civic, and political leaders, reaffirming our commitment to mutually beneficial transatlantic partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on when the Sentencing Bill will be introduced to the Assembly.
answer_text: I intend to introduce the Sentencing Bill to the Assembly before the end of this year, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the (i) quality; and (ii) security, of employment among young people aged 16 to 24.
answer_text: My department has adopted the Carnegie Framework to define work quality while focussing on the good jobs rate, a subset of indicators to monitor three areas that are fundamental to all jobs. The most recent statistics from the Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings shows that 27% of employees aged 16 to 24 years are working in jobs that meet the good jobs criteria of having a permanent, non-zero hour contract and earning at least the real living wage. The Labour Force Survey shows that almost nine out of every ten (89%) employees aged 16-24 years were in permanent employment or were in temporary employment but did not want a permanent post.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of violent attacks against prison officers on prison estates, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of assaults on prison officers in each year of the last five years is shown in the table below.YearNumber of Assaults202032202171202266202359202496Experience across prison environments is that high prison populations coupled with overcrowding are the most significant factors contributing to prison instability, incidents and violence.  The current population challenges being faced by the Northern Ireland Prison Service are therefore clearly a factor in the increasing number of assaults.Any assault on a prison officer is unacceptable and the Northern Ireland Prison Service continues to maintain a clear focus on the safety of all staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions he has taken to improve welfare priorities for dogs within council-run pounds.
answer_text: The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 empowers District Councils to establish and operate dog pounds.  Accordingly, the management of these facilities is the responsibility of each council, which is obligated to ensure that all dogs are cared for in compliance with statutory animal welfare standards as outlined in the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.  My Department has no role in directing councils as to how they fulfil their legal duties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the waiting times for the diagnosis of brain tumours.
answer_text: My department is not currently aware of any significant issues relating to waiting times for the diagnosis of brain tumours.Data from the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) on Routes to Diagnosis of Cancer (2018–2021) highlights important patterns in how brain and central nervous system (CNS) cancers are diagnosed.Emergency presentation was the most common route, accounting for an average of 101 cases per year (61.7%).Other outpatient appointments were the second most frequent route, with an average of 26 cases per year (15.9%).Red flag referrals represented a very small proportion, averaging just 1.7% of cases annually.These figures suggest that a significant proportion of brain tumour diagnoses are made at a late stage, often in emergency settings, which may reflect the rapid onset and non-specific nature of symptoms. Performance information shows that both the 31 day and 62 day cancer targets for this tumour site are consistently achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what legal requirements exist for the labelling of halal meat products, including whether producers or retailers are obliged to indicate halal certification or slaughter methods on packaging.
answer_text: Producers and retailers are not legally required to indicate halal certification or method of slaughter on packaging.  It is for religious authorities to decide if meat is halal. Any information provided on the method of slaughter must be accurate and not misleading to the consumer.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how he encourages local authorities to support local producers through food contracts for civic buildings.
answer_text: I am passionate about the quality of our local food and am keen to do everything I can to promote its consumption. The promotion of our world class Northern Ireland food and drink is a strategic priority of the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework.  I recognise that public procurement spend on food is significant and has the potential to play a key role in delivering better economic, health and environmental outcomes. Maximising public procurement opportunities on food could also bring economic benefits for food producers and businesses supplying government institutions.I recently published the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Action Plan, containing thirty-nine actions, many of which have a focus on collaboration across departments to deliver better outcomes for Northern Ireland.One of these actions is “To Support Environmentally Sustainable, Safe and Ethical Agri-Food Supply Chains”. I am delighted that to deliver against this action the Department of Finance is co-hosting an event with the Department of Justice on 1 October 2025 for food distributers and local producers to ensure a better understanding of the need for transparency in agri-food supply chains. This includes a networking and meet the buyer opportunity (including local authority representatives) to encourage shorter supply chains with local food suppliers. It is further to stimulate and encourage local producers and suppliers to participate in tendering to the public sector either individually or through the distributors supply chain. I strongly believe that by working collaboratively and sharing good practice there is even more that can be done. Local service sector providers already stock a large range of goods from Northern Ireland producers, and I would hope in the future this will increase further.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what steps his Department is taking to increase the proportion of locally grown food procured by the public sector.
answer_text: I am passionate about the quality of our local food and I am very keen to ensure that opportunities are maximised for our high-quality locally produced food, in all markets both locally and on the global stage.The promotion of our world class Northern Ireland food and drink is a strategic priority of the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework. I recently published the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Action Plan, in which one of the thirty-nine actions is focused on identifying opportunities to increase local sourcing. I am aware that a Public Sector Food Procurement Working Group has been established and good work is being done across a range of public sector organisations to identify opportunities and barriers.  The work of this group includes establishing a baseline of food procured from local businesses, develop actions to increase attractiveness of public sector procurement, identifying opportunities to build local capacity and capability and explore opportunities to make tendering more accessible.I believe that our schools, hospitals and prisons already serve a high proportion of locally sourced food, however this work will allow us to quantify this more accurately and take the necessary actions to support local food and drinks businesses who wish to supply the public sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
