question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his meeting with the Chancellor of the Exchequer on 12 August 2025.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on opportunities for North American investment in Northern Ireland since she assumed office.
answer_text: Attracting productivity-enhancing, regionally distributed inward investment is central to my department’s economic strategy for the North. I work closely with Invest NI, both locally and internationally, to promote the region’s unique investment proposition—particularly our dual market access—to prospective and existing investors.There remains strong interest from North American investors. I assumed office in February of this year and in March, I visited the US with Invest NI officials to meet senior executives, business leaders, and industry networks. The visit reinforced our commitment to transatlantic partnerships and highlighted the North’s strengths as a competitive, strategic location for US businesses.Investment decisions often take time, with multiple visits and evaluations. Even after a decision is made, it can take 3–5 years to realise the full job creation potential. Nonetheless, I am confident that Invest NI’s continued engagement with North American businesses will yield positive long-term outcomes.In more recent weeks I have welcomed the trade successes of local Belfast based businesses; The Foundation and The Creative Media Company who collectively secured new contracts worth over £200,000, while Seedling and Jo Hopkins Consulting signed distribution agreements extending their reach in the North American market.  These successes were a result of an Invest NI Trade Mission to Chicago & Toronto (September 24)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress made in integrating digital and technological solutions to reduce cost burdens and increase efficiency across the health service.
answer_text: My Department continues to seek to reduce costs and improve efficiency across all health and social care activities; digital and technological solutions included. A number of already integrated digital and technological solutions  - such as mobile tools like Rover - have streamlined community documentation and enhanced governance of care regardless of location.    The encompass programme, which has introduced a regional Electronic Patient Record (EPR) for Acute, Community and Mental Health care sectors, is one of the bigger examples of such work. The EPR, now live in all HSC Trusts, is forecast to show both quantitative and qualitative benefits to include,Estimated cash releasing benefits of up to £29.6million over 14 years, andNoncash releasing benefits value of up to £514 million, through Increased theatre utilisation, Reduction in rejected lab samples, Reduced error in charts and medication administration, Reduced delayed doses of medication, Reduction in outpatient DNAs (Did not attend), Reduction in VTEs (Venous thromboembolism) and pressure ulcers,Improved utilisation of outpatient services,Improved patient experienceEnhanced patient care and decision-making due to the ability to review patient charts from anywhere,Improved communication pathways between acute and community services leading to better resource utilisation, andImprovements in appointment booking processes that have reduced double bookings and provided a more patient centric service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the availability of self-injecting biologic medications for patients with inflammatory bowel disease.
answer_text: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) is a collective term for a number of chronic bowel disorders. The two most common forms of IBD are Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis.The following self-injected biologic medicines are approved by a recognised UK health technology approval body and are commissioned in Northern Ireland as options for treating moderate to severe IBD (including Crohn’s Disease, and Ulcerative Colitis):AdalimumabGolimumabUstekinumabVedolizumabRisankizumabMirikizumabThese medicines are Red List medicines meaning that they can only be prescribed by a hospital specialist, with dispensing arranged by hospital pharmacy teams (often involving third party ‘Homecare’ companies). These medicines cannot be prescribed by a GP, or dispensed by a community pharmacy.A typical journey for a patient with inflammatory bowel disease involves referral to a hospital specialist for diagnosis and treatment. When diagnosed, less specialist oral or topical treatments such as steroids, aminosalicylates and immunosuppressants are commonly used as first line treatments for IBD. For patients with more severe forms of the disease, or who do not respond to first line treatments, hospital specialists will consider if biological treatments (or Advanced Therapies) are a suitable next step. A range of advanced therapies with several routes of administration available, including intravenous and subcutaneous biologics. The choice of the most appropriate advanced therapy is complex, based on many patient specific factors. Patients are involved in the discussion around choice of therapy.All advanced therapies are used in line with the managed entry process for Northern Ireland, which specifies the criteria that must be met to permit the use of each medicine. This is based on Technology Appraisals from a recognised UK health technology approval body. Including the National Institute for health and Care Excellence (NICE), Scottish Medicines Consortium or All Wales Medicines Strategy Group.

answer_text: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) is a collective term for a number of chronic bowel disorders. The two most common forms of IBD are Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis.The following self-injected biologic medicines are approved by a recognised UK health technology approval body and are commissioned in Northern Ireland as options for treating moderate to severe IBD (including Crohn’s Disease, and Ulcerative Colitis):AdalimumabGolimumabUstekinumabVedolizumabRisankizumabMirikizumab(i) Clinical BarriersThere are no clinical barriers to self-injecting biologics, however, it is dependent on disease severity, patient preference, and the specific medication's suitability for self-administration.(ii) Logistical BarriersNo logistical barriers to providing availability have been identified. In line with Managed Entry processes, some medications require additional paperwork to be submitted for approval.(iii) Financial Barriers:There are no financial barriers that would prevent  patients from accessing a therapy which has been approved by a recognised UK health technology appraisal body.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) who was responsible for designing and approving the current policy in relation to the use of toilets in Parliament Buildings; (ii) the year the policy was approved; and (iii) any third-party organisations that were engaged with in the process.
answer_text: The Transgender Policy, which is a component of the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook, was approved by the Assembly Commission’s Senior Management Team (SMT) on 28 February 2023. The Transgender policy applies to Assembly Commission staff, inward secondees, agency workers and contractors.The Assembly Commission’s Equality Scheme (2022-2026) states that “Consultation will seek the views of those directly affected by the matter/policy. This may include representative groups of Section 75 categories, our staff and their Trade Unions”. Consequently, before the Transgender Policy was approved for inclusion in the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook, internal consultation with Assembly Commission staff (including inward secondees, agency workers and contractors) took place from 4 to 29 October 2021. As there were no known transgender staff within the Assembly Commission staff body at that time, views on the draft policy were also sought from a number of relevant external groups. 11 organisations were consulted during the development of the Transgender Policy, in advance of its inclusion in the Assembly Commission’s Staff Handbook. Those who provided consultation responses included Assembly Commission staff, NIPSA, HERe NI, and Focus - The Identity Trust.  Further staff and Trade Union Side internal consultation took place from 14 November to 12 December 2022. Separate ‘Guidance on Assisting and Supporting Transgender Visitors to Parliament Buildings’ was subsequently developed in September 2023 and included in the Staff Handbook.Legal advice was received during development of the Policy and Guidance and, as the Policy and Guidance involved changes to the Staff Handbook and were in keeping with the settled legislative and legal position at the time, they were approved by SMT.  The political parties represented on the Assembly Commission were not consulted. The Clerk/Chief Executive’s delegated authority required her to consult the Assembly Commission on issues which are novel, contentious and politically sensitive.  Therefore, following the UK Supreme Court’s judgment, the Clerk/Chief Executive brought this matter to the Assembly Commission and it agreed to undertake a review of the Transgender Policy and Guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the total legal costs incurred by his Department in the last five years.
answer_text: In the last five years my department has spent £3.9m on legal costs.  In April 2022 the Health and Social Care Board was closed and brought into the Department of Health as the Strategic Planning and performance Group.  Therefore, their legal costs are included in the total for the last two years.Over the past five years my department has sought legal advice relating to the Public Inquires the department are involved in, including Covid, Infected Blood, Muckamore Abbey Hospital and Urology. There are also other legal cases that we seek legal advice for which I am sure you will understand that I cannot comment on.  The expenditure is made up of payments to the Departmental Solicitors Office who provide legal representation for my department and external Counsel costs.It is necessary for the department to receive appropriate advice in relation to legal issues, as not to do so could have wider cost and other consequences.Similarly to any other costs, my department reviews and scrutinises the costs to ensure they are reasonable, and that Management of Public Money Northern Ireland guidance is adhered to.In the current financial environment, everyone is being encouraged to bear down on all costs in an effort to find savings that can be channelled to mitigate potential cuts to our citizens.It is recognised that this will be challenging to deliver as these savings are at an unprecedented level. However, all our organisations are committed to doing everything they can to deliver against the target, short of the sort of catastrophic impacts to services I have said I will not sanction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to engage with the Irish Football Association to ensure their policy on biological men competing in women's football is in keeping with the updated policy undertaken by the English Football Association and the Scottish Football Association.
answer_text: The Irish Football Association, as the National Governing Body for football in Northern Ireland, has stated it will review the judgment and guidance from the Equality and Human Rights Commission to ensure compliance with the Equality Act 2010. As with all sports, Association Football is self-governing, and this decision rests solely with the Irish Football Association. I am currently considering the implications of the judgement, which I welcome, for my own department and Arm’s Length Bodies, and I will consider issuing advice in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any engagement she has had with business stakeholders in relation to her proposed Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: In the week I made my announcement, I met twice with business and other stakeholders to update them on my proposals for the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.  My predecessor Conor Murphy also engaged extensively on the proposals with business, trade union and other stakeholders, across multiple sectors, including retail, hospitality, manufacturing and the voluntary and community sector. He met them initially in February 2024, and during the consultation period. Both he and I have met frequently with stakeholders to discuss wider Departmental issues, including these proposals. All stakeholders had the opportunity to respond to the extended consultation held by my Department.  91 responses were received from businesses and their representative organisations, including 25 industry representative bodies with a combined membership of over 18,000 individuals and businesses. My officials also met with numerous business, trade union and other stakeholders, before, during and following the consultation.  Over 30 events took place where my Department engaged specifically with business stakeholders, with hundreds of people attending.  I expect this engagement to continue. As we move forward to refine my proposals, I want to build on that constructive engagement. I am happy to continue to meet with all stakeholders to discuss this important work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide his assessment of the pre-school admissions process for 2025.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for managing the pre-school admissions process and for ensuring sufficient funded pre-school education places are available in all areas to accommodate all target age children whose parents want a place.  The process consists of two stages and is designed so that children can receive offers at either stage.  In each of the last six years, at least 99.8% of children whose parents stayed with the process to the end received an offer of a funded pre-school place in a setting of their preference.  In Stage one of the process for 2025/26 admissions, 97% children received an offer in a setting of their preference, 89% in their first preference setting.  At that point 620 children had not received an offer, however over 2,200 funded places remained available in settings across Northern Ireland.Turning to the question of Lagan Valley, the EA advised that at the end of Stage one.  31 children did not receive an offer at that time, with 86 funded places still available in the constituency. Since the end of stage one, 116 children have received an offer through the recall process, four of whom are in the Lagan Valley constituency.  The pre-school admissions process is preference based and children can only be considered by pre-school settings listed in their application.  It is, however, not possible to accommodate every child in their parents’ first preference. Parents are encouraged to consider all pre-school provision, list a number of preferences and stay with the process to the end to maximise the opportunity for their child to be offered a funded pre-school place.  The EA PEG will continually review pre-school provision and where additional funded places are required, will adjust allocations to settings accordingly.  Children on a waiting list will be offered any additional places in the order they appear on that setting’s waiting list.  Parents will be advised of the outcome of applications submitted during Stage two on Tuesday 10th June.  Any child whose parents remained with the process to the end but has not received an offer of a funded place at that stage or did not engage in stage two will still be able to access funded places by contacting settings with places still available and submitting a paper application to the setting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what plans he has to open a farm business development scheme.
answer_text: Farm Business Development support is being delivered through the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) which provides options for all farm businesses in Northern Ireland to secure and improve their viability and environmental sustainability.The SAP encompasses a wide range of schemes, some of which will provide support and assistance to farm businesses. Under the Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Transfer Scheme - Business Sustainability Groups are currently open for applications and offer the opportunity for farmers to use knowledge, co-operation and innovation to improve the sustainability and performance of their businesses.Other schemes under the SAP will serve as data platforms to provide important information to help farm businesses e.g. Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, Bovine Genetics Programme and the Carbon Footprinting Project. In addition, others will provide payments through schemes for: Farm Sustainability Payment, Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, Protein Crops Scheme, Beef Sustainability Package, Farming with Nature Package, Farming for the Generations Programme, Horticulture Schemes and Supply Chain Scheme.The Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme will aim to support investment in equipment and technology to improve farm efficiency and environmental performance. It will be available from late 2025 subject to necessary approvals and budget availability.I have agreed the timeline for the roll out of the schemes of the SAP over the next two years (subject to necessary business case agreements and legislation in place) and outlined the associated communication plan to ensure that farm businesses, and those who provide support and services to farm businesses, are fully informed of the changes ahead and the necessary management steps needed in preparation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to increase the number of appointments for people displaying signs of endometriosis.
answer_text: The Elective Overnight Stay Centre has been established at the Mater Hospital and this has enabled a reduction in diagnostic delays for endometriosis particularly for those patients waiting for diagnostic laparoscopy.My Department has also provided funding for a number of initiatives including an increase in MDT capacity to support case discussion and agreement of next steps in the patient pathway for complex gynaecology/endometriosis.  This has cleared the backlog of patients in this category.The care pathway for endometriosis will often involve stages of diagnosis and treatment and in that regard the funding for these initiatives has also targeted validation of waiting lists, additional outpatient capacity (including backlog and telephone review clinics) and additional day case and inpatient theatre lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has made an assessment of the merits of (i) reviewing; and (ii) changing the probation guidelines to prevent convicted child sex offenders from living within certain perimeters of schools and parks.
answer_text: Management of individuals convicted of sexual offences living in the community is an operational matter for the relevant agencies. PSNI deals with cases of offenders who are subject to notification requirements under the Sexual Offences Act 2003, commonly referred to as the ‘sex offenders register’. This requires offenders to notify police of certain personal details, including their address.  PSNI also manages individuals who are subject to civil prevention orders, such as the Sexual Offences Prevention Order, to manage risk from sexual harm.  Conditions can include only residing at an address that is approved by the police or prohibiting them from going to certain places such as to schools or playparks.   PBNI manages individuals who are subject to statutory supervision by in the community (for example, offenders who are on licence).  The suitability of addresses for such offenders living in the community is assessed on a case by case basis, taking into account all relevant factors.  The Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland (PPANI) bring together agencies – including PSNI, PBNI and NI Housing Executive - to manage sexual and violent offenders who pose a risk of serious harm.  These agencies take into account a range of factors when assessing suitability of accommodation for each relevant offender being managed under PPANI.  These include: whether there are children or vulnerable adults living at or frequently visiting the address; whether the accommodation is in immediate proximity to the victim’s or child centred locations; and whether there are concerns about association with other offenders in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will increase NHS dental funding to improve (i) accessibility; (ii) workforce retention; and (iii) patient outcomes.
answer_text: I acknowledge the importance of improving accessibility, workforce retention, and patient outcomes within our NHS dental services. The Department of Health is committed to enhancing General Dental Services (GDS), with recent initiatives aimed at addressing these critical areas.An investment package of £9.2m has supported GDS in 2024/25. This package includes funding aimed at increasing accessibility through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme and the Dental Access Scheme, which have facilitated the treatment of many high-priority unregistered patients. These measures are designed to improve patient outcomes by ensuring timely and effective dental treatments.Regarding workforce retention, my Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan included a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of GDS by April 2027.The first step on this path is to determine the real cost of providing dental services so that realistic options for reform of the service can be developed.To achieve that I have approved the commissioning of a cost-of-service review to be completed in 2025/26. The outworking of the review will inform the next steps, and it would be counterproductive to pre-empt the conclusion of the review by predetermining those next steps.I have made clear that I will do everything within my power to drive for better outcomes for patients across Northern Ireland. However, it is an unfortunate reality that the budget that has been allocated to me for next year is not sufficient to meets the needs of the current service, let alone introduce the kind of transformative change we all wish to see. My Department remains dedicated to ongoing dialogue with stakeholders, including the British Dental Association, to explore additional strategies for long-term improvements in Health Service dentistry. Our goal is to ensure that these services are sustainable, accessible, and capable of delivering high-quality care to all our patients.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of staff in his Department responsible for (a) processing; and (b) responding to Freedom of Information Act requests; and (ii) the annual cost of this work in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has a legal obligation to respond to Freedom of Information (FOI) requests. A small central FOI co-ordination team consisting of 1.5 FTE of staff undertakes the overall management of the FOI response process including allocation, advice, and guidance. Responses however are processed and issued by the appropriate business area, or areas, responsible for the subject of the request. As each FoI is different and requires varying levels of staff contribution my Department does not routinely track costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans her Department has in place to encourage the reuse of existing buildings to generate additional housing supply.

The detail of specific actions to be taken are being considered by my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any communication his Department has had with schools in relation to disruption due to security concerns.
answer_text: In regard to the security alert on Monday 24th March, the Education Authority and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools communicated with all impacted schools to offer advice and support.  A communication was also sent to all schools to provide factual information about the incident and to signpost schools to guidance on security, emergency planning and health and safety advice on the Education Authority website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to introduce a similar scheme to the temporary ROI Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The RoI Reimbursement Scheme, a limited administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD), was introduced for a 12-month period from 30 June 2021 (which was extended to September 2022) to help reduce Northern Ireland waiting lists.A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted during the 2024/25 budget setting process as part of a wider bid of £135m of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients in 2024/2025. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 NI Budget, as agreed by a majority of MLA’s, no additional funding was provided for tackling our waiting lists and the focus has had to be on supporting those on cancer and time critical waiting lists.The NI Executive’s Programme for Government (PfG), includes a reference to investing up to £215m a year on the waiting list backlog, including up to £135m a year for waiting list initiatives, in line with the required investment levels in the revised Elective Care Framework published last year. However, the draft 2025/26 NI budget does not contain any additional funding for waiting lists. As such, there is no funding available to reintroduce any similar reimbursement scheme. The consideration of any replacement scheme will be subject to confirmation of additional resource investment for Waiting List Initiatives. Any funding provided, must be prioritised based on maximising clinical outcomes and value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost of the independent investigation conducted by Deloitte into contractor fraud at NI Water.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water, and I can confirm the total cost of the independent investigation conducted by Deloitte into contractor fraud at NI Water is £144,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will develop regional guidance on the Gender Identity and Expression Employment Policy undertaken by each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I very much understand the argument for single sex spaces in certain circumstances, however, I also want Health and Social Care to be a welcoming and supportive place for everyone who works there, regardless of class, creed, race, or identity. I understand gender identity policies are under review at a number of HSC Trusts, which are important processes, and I will be seeking more information on that and what else may need to be done to provide greater clarity and protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Sentencing Council's Imposition of Community and Custodial Sentences applies to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Sentencing Council is the independent body responsible for developing sentencing guidelines which courts in England and Wales must follow when passing a sentence. The Sentencing Council’s remit extends only to England and Wales.  Accordingly, its  guideline on the Imposition of Community and Custodial Sentences does not apply in Northern Ireland.In Northern Ireland sentencing decisions are a matter exclusively for the independent judiciary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Sentencing Council's Imposition of Community and Custodial Sentences - Effective from 1 April 2025 guidelines, as applies to (i) faith; (ii) race; (iii) religion; (iv) sexuality and (v) gender.
answer_text: The Sentencing Council is an independent body responsible for developing sentencing guidelines which courts in England and Wales must follow when passing a sentence. The courts in Northern Ireland are not required to follow or take account of the Sentencing Council’s guidelines, though they may choose to do so.  Sentencing guidance and sentencing decisions in Northern Ireland are exclusively matters for the independent judiciary.  Accordingly, I have not made an assessment of the Sentencing Council’s guideline on the imposition of community and custodial sentences.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the measures in the Programme for Government 2024-2027 to end violence against women and girls.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the length of time it currently takes for child sexual abuse cases to go to trial.
answer_text: Cases involving sexual offences are generally amongst those with the longest case processing times. This is due to a number of factors including case complexity and that such cases are more likely to proceed to a full trial.Recent data indicates that there was approximately an 11% improvement in the average time taken to complete sexual offence cases between 2022-23 and 2023-24.  The average time for these cases in 2023-24 was 675 days, a decrease from 757 days in 2022-23. Statistics show that cases are being completed faster than they were pre-Covid, down from 698 days in 2019-20.  Whilst a positive step forward, I and my justice colleagues acknowledge that there is much more to be done.Efforts to improve case processing times in child sexual abuse cases include a judge-led voluntary protocol to expedite serious sexual offence cases for child complainants aged under 16. My Department is working with judiciary and criminal justice partners to further develop this protocol.More broadly, the Criminal Justice Board has prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay within the system benefiting victims and witnesses including those involved in sexual offence cases. This work is built around five key workstreams and will take forward existing work on committal reform, existing and emerging work in the digital arena and continue to drive forward other areas for efficiencies such as early engagement, court remits and out of court disposals. I am pleased that Executive colleagues have recognised the importance of this issue by agreeing to include the Speeding up Justice Programme within the Safer Communities priority in the Programme for Government (PfG) and that the Programme has recently been awarded funding through the interim Public Sector Transformation funding.Justice is demand led, meaning that funding must go to front line services first and, given our significant budgetary pressures, this means that scope for innovation, up until now, has been limited.  However, the successful Transformation funding bid will provide the opportunity to make significant progress with Speeding Up and Transforming the Criminal Justice System. I appreciate there is much more we need to do to improve case processing times in child sexual abuse cases, and cases in general, and I along with my justice partners are committed to making the changes needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has had any communication with social media platforms in relation to their responsibility for monitoring the sexual grooming of children on their applications.
answer_text: Telecommunications, including the regulation and monitoring of social media platforms, is a reserved matter, and therefore responsibility rests with UK Government.I welcomed the introduction by the UK Government of the Online Safety Act 2023 and was broadly content with it. Ofcom are the regulator for online harms on social media platforms and I engage with them, taking a proactive approach towards supporting their work on online safety.  The role of Ofcom in monitoring and enforcement will be vital to ensure compliance from social media platforms and to protect children from online harm.  In particular I have urged for more to be done to address the lack of accountability of anonymous account holders, a key issue when seeking to prevent and detect online harms and offences.Further information in relation to Online Safety can be found in the response to AQW 23778/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the progress made since 2022 in relation to introducing reforms to reduce the time it takes to complete criminal cases.
answer_text: It is pleasing that case processing times have improved by 16% over the past two years.  The latest Departmental statistics for 2023-24 indicate that the average (median) time taken for a case to be dealt with, from date the offence was reported to police, was 190 days, compared to 226 days in 2021-22.  This is a key measure of how the system is performing overall, and a sign that efforts to speed up the justice system are having an impact.There have been a number of positive developments since 2022.  The first phase of Committal Reform was implemented in October 2022. Where the option of calling victims and witnesses to provide oral evidence during committal proceedings in the magistrates’ court was abolished, helping to reduce stress placed on some victims and witnesses who may have been call to provide oral evidence more than once. The Department is presently working with criminal justice partners to bring forward the second phase of committal reform which relates to the direct committal of certain cases to the Crown Court.More broadly, the Criminal Justice Board (CJB), which features the key senior leaders working within the justice system, has prioritised a programme of work, based on five workstreams, each led by a CJB member, which will seek to reform the way in which cases are handled, getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, and reducing the demands on courts and the wider system.  This work supports the Executive’s Programme for Government priority to deliver Safer Communities in which the public can live productively and engage fully in society. This work is supported by a performance reporting framework which enables my officials to work with justice colleagues to identify and address potential blockages in the system. Progress has been made across each of the workstreams, however, the pace and scope of change will be greatly dependent on funding.  Some projects may require legislation and it will take time to embed new processes and progress work for long-term change.My Department has been successful in its bid for transformation funding of £20.45 million for Speeding up Justice and Transforming the Criminal Justice. This will mainly enable essential work to progress on efforts to improve Early Engagement between the key parties to help drive forward cases, and on expanding the use of Out of Courts disposals to help divert more cases involving low level offending away from the courts system.However more needs to be done, particularly in respect of the more serious cases heard in the Crown Court which can take significantly longer.  The criminal justice system in Northern Ireland is presently under significant pressure, and is still addressing case backlogs created as a result of the Covid pandemic.  The number of cases prosecuted through the courts remains high and the recent action by the Criminal Bar Association will exacerbate existing backlogs and will in due course have a detrimental impact on case processing times,

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to review the maximum penalties available in the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008.
answer_text: There are already significant maximum penalties available to the judiciary when sentencing for serious sexual offences under the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 (the 2008 Order). These penalties sit appropriately within the Northern Ireland criminal legislative framework and also align with the maximum penalties available to the judiciary in England and Wales under the Sexual Offences Act 2003. The 2008 Order is kept under regular review and updated with new offences to deal with emerging criminal behaviour. For example, the 2008 Order was amended by the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 to criminalise upskirting, down-blousing, cyber-flashing and sexual grooming: pretending to be a child. The scope of the existing abuse of trust offences was widened to include the areas of sport and religion.  Sentencing within the legislative framework in individual cases is a matter for the independent judiciary. I have also recently tasked officials to commence a scoping exercise into a potential review of sentences and sentencing frameworks for serious sexual offences. Whilst the scoping exercise is at a very early stage, I anticipate that it will seek to draw on any lessons emerging from the Sentencing Review currently underway in England and Wales as well as from comparative datasets on available maxima and average sentences across these islands for similar offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any ongoing work to reduce the time it takes to complete criminal cases.
answer_text: Tackling delay is one of the biggest challenges facing the criminal justice system and is a key priority for the Department and relevant justice agencies. However, reducing the time it takes to complete criminal cases is a challenging and complex issue.  Covid has had a long term significant impact on the criminal justice system with case volumes remaining higher than prior to Covid.  Whilst some progress is being made in relation to case processing times, the recent withdrawal of service by the Criminal Bar Association will create further backlogs which, without additional funding, will cause further delays. The delivery of a Speeding up Justice Programme is a key element of the department’s commitments to help deliver Safer Communities under the new Programme for Government.  The programme will seek to reform the way in which criminal cases are handled.  This includes getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, bringing forward proposals regarding the sentencing jurisdiction of the courts, enhancing our digital capabilities, and finding better, more efficient ways of dealing with low level offending behaviour.The Speeding up Justice Programme features five workstreams. Progress has been made across each of the workstreams.  A brief update on the priorities for each workstream is provided below.The Department is working with criminal justice partners to take forward the second phase of the Committal Reform workstream (direct committal), which aims to remove the traditional committal process entirely for the most serious offences, with the majority of cases being directly transferred to the Crown Court at the first court appearance in the magistrates’ court.  The Early Engagement workstream, which is led by the Public Prosecution Service (PPS), focuses on how key stakeholders such as police, prosecution, defence and courts can work more collaboratively at earlier points in the criminal justice process to deliver more timely case outcomes.  This work is being taken forward in three phases which aims to support for more effective engagement between PSNI and PPS, the prosecution and defence, and also support the management of cases through the court system.    As part of ongoing work on the Out Of Court Disposals workstream, led by PSNI, the department is preparing a consultation on proposals to expand the use of out of courts disposals to divert more cases of low level offending away from the court system.  These proposals aim to deliver speedier outcomes to low level cases helping to improve efficiency and reduce pressure on the criminal justice system, ensuring more resources are available to tackle more serious offending behaviour and keep the community safe.  The proposals seek to build on recent developments such as the expansion of Fixed Penalty Notice powers to allow police to issue an FPN for Careless driving to provide an alternative to prosecuting those cases through the courts, and changes to the Community Resolution Notice sch

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will give consideration to instructing planners to condition the requirement for bat surveys on planning approvals.
answer_text: My Department’s role is advisory. It has no powers to instruct planners to condition the requirement for bat surveys on planning approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures in place to ensure victims of sexual violence and abuse are supported in schools when suffering from psychological trauma.
answer_text: There are no specific measures in place under the joint Department of Education and Department of Health Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework to provide support in schools for victims of sexual violence and abuse.  The Framework provides guidelines for all schools to promote positive emotional health and wellbeing and encourages all schools to adopt a whole school approach to emotional health and wellbeing. Alongside the Framework projects, the Education Authority (EA) provides the post-primary school counselling service (HOPE) to all post-primary schools. The EA is also commissioned to provide support for the implementation of Operation Encompass, an early intervention partnership between the police and educational settings that focuses on information-sharing in relation to domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her work to strengthen protections against the online sexual abuse of children.
answer_text: Keeping children safe online is a shared responsibility across all government departments. This is reflected in the Executive’s 5 Year Online Safety Strategy and accompanying action plan, which aims to ensure people can participate in the online world in a positive, safe and responsible way. However, telecommunications, including the regulation of social media platforms, is a reserved matter and therefore responsibility rests with the UK Government.The Executive’s Online Safety Strategy was published in February 2021.  The associated 3-year Action Plan is largely proceeding as planned, we are currently in year 3.  The work is led by the Online Safety Co-ordinator at the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI), and my officials are represented on a cross-departmental implementation group (CDIG), chaired by the Department of Health, which oversees implementation of actions.The SBNI have commissioned the National Children’s Bureau (NCB) to undertake an online safety training needs analysis for the children’s workforce which will help to understand the current experience of learning and development in online safety and identify future training and development needs. Findings from this survey will also be used to inform the development of actions for years 4 & 5.The Online Safety Act 2023 introduced new UK-wide laws that protects both children and adults from online harm by creating a new regulatory framework for online platforms and search services. It puts the onus on service providers to take measures to protect all users from illegal content and to also protect children from harmful content and activity.The online independent regulator, Ofcom, is responsible for the implementation of the new regime.  Ofcom has published extensive plans for the development codes of practice and guidance for providers, some which are designed to specifically protect children. Further information can be found in Ofcom’s policy statement here.I am currently engaging with the Westminster government with a view to extending particular offences in the Crime and Policing Bill to Northern Ireland, through a Legislative Consent Motion which will be subject to Assembly approval. This includes offences relating to child sexual abuse material, including possession of advice or guidance about creating paedophile manuals, and the use of child sexual abuse image-generators.The Northern Ireland Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031 launched on the 25 September. The strategy is led by the Departments of Justice and Health but covers the responsibilities of a number of Northern Ireland departments. In addition to the existing pillars on Partnership, Prevention, Provision and Justice, the Strategy now includes a fifth pillar specifically focussed on meeting the needs of children and young people, including online sexual abuse. My Department’s Modern Slavery Human Trafficking and Child Exploitation Branch engaged on issues concerning child exploitation and traffickin

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the amount spent by (a) his Department; and (b) his Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the anticipated spend by (a) his Department; and (b) his Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training in the 2024-25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the amount spent by (a) her Department; and (b) her Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the anticipated spend by (a) her Department; and (b) her Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Staff within the Department of Justice can access e-learning courses on diversity through Learning, Information, Knowledge and Skills (LInKS), the Northern Ireland Civil Service wide system for learning and development. It is not possible to provide a cost specifically for those courses.Diversity training for Northern Ireland Prison Service staff is delivered by tutors at the Prison Service College (PSC) and therefore any associated costs are included within the operational costs of the PSC. It is therefore not possible to provide a cost specifically for diversity training.Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI) spent £2,080 in 2021-22 on an e-learning course for all PBNI staff on Equality & Diversity. PBNI now have online training courses in the area of Diversity as part of their online learning management system. It is not possible to provide the direct cost of these modules.There has been no further spend by my Department or its arm’s length bodies on diversity training in each of the last five years, and there is no anticipated spend on diversity training in the 2024-25 financial year, beyond what is available as outlined above.My Department does not hold information in relation to spend or anticipated spend on diversity training by the following Non-Departmental Public Bodies of my Department, given their operational independence which is established in legislation: Police Service of Northern Ireland;Northern Ireland Policing Board; andOffice of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland.You may, therefore, wish to contact them directly to request the information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is taking to present Northern Ireland as a tourist destination to travel industry professionals in North America.
answer_text: Tourism Ireland (TI) is undertaking an extensive programme of marketing activity throughout 2025, to continue to grow business from the United States. This includes targeting influential American travel trade, including tour operators, airlines, travel agents, travel advisors and online travel agents (OTAs.	TI will undertake sales missions to the US in the first half of 2025, meeting and doing business with hundreds of travel advisors in key cities. They will develop a series of familiarisation visits to Ireland for travel advisors and tour operators, alongside a presence at key travel trade events in the United States throughout the year. They are recruiting top US tour operators to participate in the annual Meet the Buyer 2025 workshop, which will take place in Lisburn and Belfast at the end of March. TI also runs a monthly programme of webinars for US travel advisors. A webinar dedicated to the north is scheduled for August 2025.They are planning the roll out of co-operative campaigns with Aer Lingus, Delta and United Airlines, to promote flights from 21 US cities to Ireland, and drive business outside of the peak season. In addition, Tourism Ireland’s golf promotional programme for 2025 kicked off in January at the PGA Show in Orlando, Florida, attracting around 30,000 golf professionals, golf industry executives and journalists, with top golf courses in the north represented. On 12 March, during my first visit to the USA as Economy Minister, I will meet with approximately 20 key US travel trade partners (including airlines and tour operators), to build relationships and connections at a TI roundtable event in New York.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs which body has the overall authority to make a typing decision on dogs.
answer_text: Councils have statutory responsibility for enforcing the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (The Dogs Order).  Under The Dogs Order, councils authorise and empower dog wardens to identify and determine if dogs are of a prohibited type.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is currently taking to strengthen legislation for perpetrators of animal cruelty.
answer_text: My officials are currently drafting a number of public consultations for my consideration. One, on the introduction of mandatory CCTV in slaughterhouses, is expected to launch in the coming weeks.  Two, on proposals to regulate the sales of pups and kittens, will be published later in the year.		Additionally, I am actively considering my wider animal welfare priorities and have been meeting with key stakeholders to outline proposals for legislative change. Following completion of these meetings, I intend to publish an animal welfare pathway which will detail my priority areas for reform for the remainder of the mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any training that has been provided to local council dog wardens in relation to the typing of dogs, including (i) whether the training was mandatory for dog wardens; (ii) whether the training was taken up by all local councils; and (iii) whether any additional funding was provided for training.
answer_text: My Department provided funding to support councils with the implementation of the XL Bully legislation, which included the training of dog wardens. Training was delivered by the Metropolitan Police Service (Met Police) for 13 dog wardens, from each council area, in October 2024. This training is broadly considered to be the highest standard available in the UK and replicates the provision undertaken by Dog Legislation Officers across the police forces of England and Wales. I have made additional funding available to enable a further cohort of dog wardens to undertake similar training.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) diversity officers; and (ii) equality officers currently employed by his Department.
answer_text: Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 requires public authorities, in carrying out their functions relating to Northern Ireland, to have due regard to the need to promote equality of opportunity and good relations across the categories of the local population outlined within the Act. The Equality and Human Rights Unit in the Department of Health supports the department in meeting its obligations under the Act.  This primarily involves fulfilling the Department's obligations under its Equality Scheme. For example, assessing, monitoring and publishing the impact of policies, conducting an Audit of Inequalities and developing associated action plans, providing staff training, and dealing with any complaints arising from a failure to comply with the scheme. There are approximately 2.6 whole time equivalent staff directly involved in the work of the Equality and Human Rights Unit in the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail her Department's vision for boosting tourist visits to Northern Ireland for the incoming year.
answer_text: In January, my predecessor launched the Tourism Vision and Action Plan outlining 17 actions to drive sustainable, regionally balanced economic growth in the sector. Delivery will be overseen by the Tourism Partnership Board.Actions for 2025 include incorporating local regions into Failte Ireland’s regional brands; Development of Destination Stewardship Plans; and Restoring appropriate funding to Tourism Ireland to increase tourism revenue from overseas. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the in-year data sickness absence costs to the Northern Ireland Civil Service for 2024-2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the historical clerical child abuse research study.
answer_text: We recognise the devastating impact that clerical child abuse has had on many people. Work is progressing on three separate pieces of research: one examining records held by faith organisations and statutory agencies; one recording experiences and expectations of victims and survivors; and one examining the safeguarding policies and practices of faith groups.We issued a Press Release on 8 January highlighting the work and calling for victims and survivors to come forward to participate in the research.   Members will also have received an email informing them of this work. This research is an important step in deciding the best way to address the impact and legacy of this abuse.  We look forward to the conclusion of the research projects in the Spring, and subsequently seeing the final reports and recommendations from the Interdepartmental Working Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to improve road safety on Killycomaine Road, Portadown.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  I can confirm that an assessment for a controlled crossing has been completed on Killycomain Road, near to Portadown College and also close to Upper Church Lane. They will both be considered for inclusion in a future works programme, taking into account my Department’s available resources and scheme priorities.A part-time 20mph speed limit has already been introduced on the Killycomain Road outside Portadown College.  This measure is aimed at increasing driver awareness and road safety in the vicinity of the school.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 telephone number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on efforts to ensure timely street lighting repairs in the Southern Division.
answer_text: My Department has allocated funding to support a comprehensive street lighting maintenance service across all four DfI Roads Divisions including Southern Division. This funding ensures that outage repairs are generally completed within the established target of five working days.I can also advise approximately 80% of the Department's sodium lighting stock has been replaced with energy-efficient LED units. This transition has significantly reduced electricity consumption, carbon emissions, and the frequency of street lighting outages, due to the longer lifespan of LEDs.  I remain committed to securing funding to complete the conversion of the remaining network at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering the use of AI to assist with referrals, testing and results to improve efficiency.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Generative AI technologies offer opportunities to improve both productivity of the existing HSC workforce and the quality-of-care services delivered, with benefits including the freeing up of capacity to enhance services through improved communications and tailored treatment plans.Future plans include testing AI algorithms to assist with referral/ decision making, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage of reporting, and results acknowledgement/alerts.However, the potential capabilities of this technology must be set against the potential risks – for instance in the areas of clinical efficacy and information governance. In these areas the Department of Health and the Health and Social Care (HSC) system needs to ensure that the tools provided are trustworthy and do not breach the privacy of patients or service users.Additionally, the wide adoption of advanced technologies like AI/GenAI require a number of underlying enablers – including good quality, codified data that describes a service users needs and historic episodes and treatments (for training purposes), the clinical safety and ethical governance structures that are required to oversee the safe introduction of these AI tools, and skills in cloud services engineering and security (recognising that the compute power required to run AI algorithms is often best provided from cloud technologies). The Department of Health and its partners in the HSC are establishing structures to develop these enablers and explore the capabilities of providers and products, with a particular focus in the first instance on medical image analysis for diagnostic purposes, an area with growing evidence to support clinical effectiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of road gritting on school bus routes, following recent incidents involving school transport.
answer_text: My Department carries out preventative treatments on the network of roads selected for salting, known as the primary salting network. This comprises main routes carrying more than 1,500 vehicles per day; and other busy routes with special difficulties – (i.e.) those carrying between 1,000 and 1,500 vehicles per day where there are special difficulties such as railway crossings, their height above sea level, extent and severity of gradients and routes linking to a small settlement containing 100 dwellings or more.In applying the criteria, a higher weighting is given to buses on the route with a 40 seater bus counted as 40 vehicles.  In this way, significant consideration is given to the impact of school bus routes on the application of this criteria.Secondary salting is the salting of any roads that are not on the salting schedule. A secondary action takes place subject to the availability of resources, once the condition of the primary route network is assured. This secondary salting will only be undertaken during particularly severe and prolonged hazardous weather conditions. Currently there are 62 rural schools on the list for secondary salting.Adding all school bus routes to the primary salted network cannot be achieved easily as the current salting routes on the primary network are optimised for efficiency in terms of time and salt used.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress relating to the negotiations between his Department and the development consortium for the completion of Phase 3 of the North Lisburn Feeder Road.
answer_text: The North Lisburn Feeder Road is a developer led scheme associated with residential development in north Lisburn. There is an Article 122 Agreement in place between the North Lisburn Development Consortium (NLDC) and my Department to facilitate the acquisition of the final piece of land required, by using the Department’s vesting powers, if necessary, to complete the scheme between the B101 at Lady Wallace Avenue joining the Pond Park Road just north of Old Park Road. Timescales for delivery of this scheme are dictated by the North Lisburn Development Consortium (NLDC). Whilst there is an Article 122 Agreement in place between the NLDC and my Department to facilitate the acquisition of the final piece of land required, the costs associated with acquisition rest with the NLDC.Another meeting with the NLDC will take place in early February.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current status of the Community Ownership Fund as pertaining to Northern Ireland, following the Autumn Budget 2024.
answer_text: The £150 million Community Ownership Fund supports community groups here and in Britain to take ownership of assets and amenities at risk of being lost. Voluntary and community groups can bid for funding to acquire important assets and run them for the benefit of the local community.The fund will target a minimum of £4.3m.After the Autumn Statement the British Government recognised the uncertainty around the fund post March 2025 and has stated plans will be confirmed in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following endorsement of NICE TA 943, hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in type 1 diabetes, as applicable in Northern Ireland, to detail (i) why a decision on its implementation has yet to be made; and (ii) when a decision will be made.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, as applicable in Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications of this decision.  This includes overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, including financial considerations given scale of investment needed. Funding is required for the devices as well as resources to upskill and recruit the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  This will inform final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI across all patient cohorts and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how much of an uptake there has been in Pension Credit applications since changes were announced to the eligibility criteria for Winter Fuel Payments.
answer_text: Since the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment eligibility were announced in July by the Chancellor of the Exchequer, the number of Pension Credit applications received by my Department has increased by 74% when compared with the number of applications received in August and September last year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on actions taken in relation to the implementation of the findings of the High Street Task Force Delivering a 21st Century High Street report.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the actions they are taking to streamline the application process for victims and survivors applying to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the methodology used to calculate average waiting times for patient referrals displayed on the My Waiting Times NI webpage.
answer_text: The reporting of average waiting times is calculated based on patients who are actively waiting for their appointment or procedure, having being referred by either a GP or clinician, which is the same methodology applied in NHS England.  This methodology ensures a comparative analysis and consistent approach to reporting active average waiting time information to patients and healthcare professionals.  Waiting times will fluctuate depending on the degree of sub specialisation, demand for services and variations in clinical urgency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on actions taken to increase provision of domiciliary care packages.
answer_text: Domiciliary Care has an absolutely key role within our Health and Social Care System, with 6% of our population aged over 65 in receipt of it. There are around 295,000 hours of domiciliary care delivered each week in Northern Ireland, with my department investing over £226m annually. In Northern Ireland, like all other healthcare regions across the UK, Domiciliary Care services are under acute pressure with high demand and limited capacity.  NISRA projections estimate that between mid-2018 and mid-2043, the population aged 65 and over will increase by over 50%.Working in partnership with the independent sector and the Trusts, service users and carers, through the Social Care Collaborative Forum, has led to initiatives including earlier and more regular reviews of care packages and putting a digital system in place across the Trusts to drive efficiency.  I recognise that supporting the workforce is a critical factor in increasing the availability of care packages. To this end, there has been recent investment of £70m to support social care providers.  This investment includes an increase in the regional funding rate for domiciliary care providers to £20.01 per hour. To maintain the workforce providers, continue to run rolling recruitment programmes for Domiciliary care workers.  In addition, my department is working in partnership with the Northern Ireland Social Care Council, to develop the training, career and qualification pathways for the domiciliary care workforce.In addition, a Social Care Fair Work Forum has also been established which will seek to embed Fair Work initiatives and improve the terms and conditions for everyone employed across all parts of the social care sectorI say all this, recognising the extremely challenging financial position that the Health and Social Care system faces. However, I have to caveat any increase in Domiciliary Care provision will be dependent upon the funding being available to achieve it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the May 2023 RCPath Consulting Report for the Southern Health and Social Care Trust on the cervical screening review.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) commissioned the Royal College of Pathologists’ (RCPath) to undertake an assessment of performance issues in cervical cytology screening from 1 January 2008 and October 2021, following concerns raised regarding its screening performance.A report by the RCPath was published, by the Southern Trust, on Monday 9th October 2023 and contained eight recommendations: six in relation to the Trust’s internal performance management and quality assurance of it’s cervical screening service; one recommendation for the implementation of primary HPV testing into the NI Cervical Screening Programme; and lastly, the RCPath recommended that a review of the previous cytology held was not undertaken due to capacity issues across the UK to deliver this and instead strongly recommended that primary HPV was introduced, with consideration of early invitation of those women considered to be most at risk. The Southern Trust fully accepted the eight recommendations made by RCPath and the Trust has worked with the Public Health Agency (PHA) and my Department to implement these.  Following careful consideration of the RCPath recommendations, PHA and SHSCT decided, with the planned introduction of primary HPV testing (implemented 11th December 2023), to undertake a precautionary review of cytology slide results as there would be a substantial fall in demand for cytology slide reviews in Northern Ireland as part of the normal Cervical Screening Programme. This, in turn, would release significant cytology capacity across the laboratories to assist in SHSCT’s review of the cytology cervical smear tests (referred to as ‘slides’) to be carried out. While the Cervical Cytology Review has been slower than initially anticipated, I am able to inform members that 79% of the review is now complete and the main review should be completed by the end of the summerI want to assure women that Northern Ireland’s cervical screening programme remains highly effective overall. It is essential that women keep coming forward for their cervical smear test when invited to do so as this will continue to save lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 8203/22-27, AQW 8204/22-27 and AQW 8205/22-27, whether he will develop an approach to the textile industry, similar to the Scottish textiles sector strategy 10 Year Plan.
answer_text: Whilst the Department has no plans to develop an approach to the textile industry, similar to the Scottish textiles sector’s strategic 10 Year Plan, the Department will continue to support the sector through Invest NI who offer a wide range of support including but not limited to Innovation and R&D, technical, skills, job growth support, marketing, trade, supply chain, green economy and access to finance.   From 2018-19 to 2022-23 Invest NI has also offered £4.98 million to businesses in the textiles sector (defined using the UK Standard Industrial Classification).The Department will be bringing forward its Circular Economy Strategy for Executive approval in the coming months. The Strategy recognises the importance of textiles, capturing what is currently being done and sets out many opportunities to improve the sectors’ environmental performance.  Although textiles has been identified as a focus area and offers great potential, it is not one of the priority sectors identified for immediate short term intervention. The three priority sectors identified are construction, manufacturing, and agriculture which cumulatively account for 70% of the materials used in our economy and contribute to nearly halve of our carbon footprint. In the longer term the Department we will work with DAERA and other stakeholders to bring forward interventions that improve sustainability across all the focus areas including textiles, electronics, packaging, and tourism.  DAERA is also currently supporting the sector through providing grant funding to the Northern Ireland Resources Network (NIRN) and Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful (KNIB) to promote waste prevention, including textile waste.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether intended changes to the NHS constitution in relation to gender designation will take effect in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), England’s consultation on its NHS Constitution, which ends on 25 June 2024.  DHSC will use the feedback from the consultation to work out how best to reflect proposed changes in the Constitution. My Department will consider any potential implications it may have in respect of the provision of gender identity services in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the NHS’s policy in relation to single-sex accommodation rights for patients.
answer_text: The privacy and dignity of all patients is of paramount consideration for those who deliver and commission care. It is crucial to ensure that patients are treated with dignity, kindness, compassion, courtesy, respect, understanding and honesty.The current guidance, which was issued by the Chief Nursing Officer in September 2016, outlines that the overriding principle is that all patients in adult inpatient areas should be cared for in same gender accommodation except where it is in the overall best interests of the patient or reflects their personal choice. Increasing pressures on bed capacity can present challenges for Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) to be able to ensure that all patients are cared for in single gender accommodation. Where this is not possible it is expected that there will be measures in place to protect each individual patient’s privacy and dignity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on actions to increase provision for domiciliary care in the Southern Heath and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust is currently providing a greater number of Domiciliary Care hours per year than it is funded to do so.  The Trust continues to work to increase provision through a number of approaches, including:The Early Review Team Pilot, which increases the frequency of service user review, to ensure that a person has the right package of care or if they don’t require services that this is offered to someone on the unmet need list.Contributing to the Regional work in respect of Domiciliary Care Services within the work of the Social Care Collaborative. The implementation of a digital live system to manage in-house domiciliary care which will make the most efficient use of the resources allocated to the Trust for Domiciliary Care services Continuing to implement a range of approaches to recruitment, to ensure that there is steady access to new recruits to replace staff retirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the progress of funding initiatives as part of the City and Growth Deals.
answer_text: There has been significant progress across the four City and Growth Deals. Positive milestones have already been achieved this year, with more to come.The Belfast Region City Deal is in delivery phase. Legally binding Contracts for Funding have been signed for nine projects, with a further six due in the coming months. Planning permission has already been granted for some projects and construction has started on the Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Centre in Newtownabbey. The Derry City and Strabane District Council City Deal is preparing for Deal signing in summer 2024. The Causeway Coast and Glens Growth Deal signed Heads of Terms last week, and it will now begin work on Outline Business Cases to progress towards Deal signing.The Mid South West Growth Deal is working toward signing Heads of Terms in autumn 2024.To date, £9.1 million has been drawn down, with an anticipated spend of £56.8 million in the 2024-25 financial year. This includes City Deal funding from both the Executive and the British Government, as well as Inclusive Future Fund and Complementary Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress made on the Portadown Flood Alleviation Scheme.
answer_text: The Portadown Flood Alleviation scheme is currently at the detailed design stage and work is ongoing to complete the design and finalise the business case. This work includes refining aspects of the proposed defences following completion of tree surveys with a view to minimising the environmental impact of the works. An addendum to the Environmental Statement for the scheme has been prepared to incorporate an additional flood defence site which was identified after the consultation on the original Environmental Statement last year. Consultation on the addendum will commence on April 26th and close on May 27th. Subject to gaining the necessary approvals and the availability of funding, it is currently estimated that the procurement process to appoint a contractor for the works could commence in Autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that a reduction in the compensation rate for cattle removed under the bovine TB programme has on small family farms.
answer_text: Last September, the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland directed my Department to consult on a proposal to reduce the compensation rate for cattle removed under the bovine Tuberculosis programme. The consultation sought views on reducing the amount paid for each bovine animal slaughtered from the current 100% of market value paid per animal, to 90% of market value paid in year 1 followed by a further reduction to 75% in year 2. Based upon an overall compensation cost of £38 million in the 2022/23 financial year, this could, if implemented, result in a total cost to farm businesses of £3.8 million in the first year of implementation rising to £9.5 million in the second year.  As the percentage paid per animal slaughtered would apply equally across all farm businesses, there has not been any separate consideration of the impact on smaller farms.  The consultation has now closed with 4,945 responses received, the overwhelming majority of which were opposed to the proposal.  I would like to thank everyone who responded to the consultation.  Mr Speaker, I am acutely aware of the devastating impact that a TB breakdown can have, and the financial challenges this causes for farmers and farming families.  I therefore appreciate that any proposal which suggests reducing compensation is difficult for the farming community.  However, given the pressures arising from bovine TB on both farmers and my department, I have to give consideration to the sustainability of our disease control programmes, which are essential to our trade in cattle and cattle products. Brian Dooher, as new Chief Veterinary Officer, has therefore been tasked to take a fresh look at the issue of bovine TB. I will be working closely with veterinary and policy officials over the time ahead on next steps for the bTB Eradication Strategy, in addition to engaging with stakeholders to examine the best ways to help lower the rate of bovine TB in our herds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to remove the requirement for drivers, who passed their driving test after 01 January 1997, to sit an additional test to tow a trailer or caravan weighing up to 3,500 kg, similar to that of the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500kg Maximum Authorized Mass (MAM).  This change was made in an effort to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales.At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed.In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes.
