question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what practical measures he is taking to support hospitality businesses with rising energy costs.
answer_text: The large fluctuations in energy prices over the past number of years, driven by our continued reliance on importing fossil fuels, highlights the importance of our local Energy Strategy ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’.   One of the key objectives of the strategy is to ensure that all businesses have access to essential and affordable energy to enable an adequate standard of competitiveness. The focus is now on priorities for delivery to ensure that we maximise the benefits for our society and economy as soon as possible.In addition, Invest NI delivers a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable NI businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies.  This includes fully funded Technical Consultancy and a Resource Matching Service. This support helps businesses to optimise and futureproof their operations by implementing green efficiencies to achieve carbon savings and cost savings to mitigate the impact of rising energy costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what legislative or policy measures she is taking to address an increase in instances of sexual grooming of children and young people.
answer_text: While Police Service of NI statistics in respect of recorded crimes of sexual grooming of children and young people show a decline since 2020/21, statistics in relation to indecent images of children (IIOC) offences demonstrate an increase in this specific type of offence. The prevention of, and protection from, child exploitation, which includes sexual grooming, is a complex matter requiring input from a range of statutory and non-statutory partners. There is a comprehensive and robust framework of law on sexual offences in place in Northern Ireland which includes a range of offences aimed at protecting children.  Included within the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 (“the 2008 Order”) are the offences of arranging or facilitating the commission of a child sex offence, meeting a child following sexual grooming and sexual communication with a child.  I introduced measures to strengthen the offences within the 2008 Order through amendment made in the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 which are unique to this jurisdiction, and which came into operation on 27 November 2023.   These are, specifically, four offences of an adult pretending to be a child for the purposes of child grooming which seek to address behaviour at an earlier stage in the child grooming process, where the offender masquerades as a child as a precursor to grooming or carrying out other offences, and where this behaviour would constitute an indicator that they present a risk to children. The offences capture all types of communication to include online communication.The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) has been at the forefront in Northern Ireland of developing evidence-based understanding and service provision for young people at risk of and experiencing exploitation.  Research funded by the Programme, “From Contextual to Criminal Harm: Young People’s Perceptions and Experiences of Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) in Northern Ireland” (March 2023), explored young people’s perceptions and experiences of criminal exploitation and partners in the voluntary and community sector.  Exposure to violence, including being witness to and/or a victim of it, appeared to be a normal part of life for the young participants, and had become so normalised that even when they had experienced harm, they often failed to recognise their victimisation.The Child Protection Senior Officials Group (CPSOG) provides cross-Departmental strategic direction on existing and emerging child protection issues. CPSOG is chaired by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice is a core member of the Group. In 2023, CPSOG agreed a two-year action plan to address child criminal exploitation in NI. This work builds on the work of EPPOC to create a system wide response to child exploitation.  The Department’s Modern Slavery, Human Trafficking and Child Exploitation Branch has engaged on issues concerning child sexual exp

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what practical measures are in place to support people affected by sexual crime.
answer_text: There is a range of practical measures in place to support people affected by sexual crime. My Department provides funding to support the provision of a number of services that offer practical help and support to victims. These include: the provision of advocacy services by ASSIST NI to victims of domestic and/or sexual abuse who are engaging with the criminal justice system, with the Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conference process (MARAC), or with the Rowan Sexual Assault Referral Centre;the provision, by Victim Support NI (VSNI) of a Children’s & Young Person’s Independent Sexual Violence Advocate (ChISVA) Service which provides advocacy and support to children and young people up to the age of 18 who have been victims of sexual violence; andthe provision by Nexus of a confidential 24 hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline which provides information, advice and support to all those affected by domestic and sexual abuse across Northern Ireland.  The Helpline is funded jointly by the Departments of Justice, Health and Communities and  is available 24 hours a day, 365 days a year.I have also appointed a Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime to represent the needs and interests of all victims of crime and who also has a specific focus on particularly vulnerable groups of victims, including victims of sexual crime.In addition, a range of measures delivered under the Gillen Review into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences are delivering practical support to victims of sexual crime. These include:the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) service, which was launched in April 2021, and which provides free, independent legal advice to adult complainants in serious sexual offence cases. The scheme has provided advice to over 1,700 complainants and feedback has been overwhelmingly positive;the establishment of Remote Evidence Centres (RECs) in Belfast and Craigavon, in addition to the existing NSPCC-led facility in Derry/Londonderry.  Vulnerable victims and witnesses are able to give their evidence by video link in the RECs without going to a courthouse, thereby removing the possibility that they might encounter the defendant or their supporters and reducing trauma for they may experience; a judge-led voluntary protocol is also in place to expedite serious sexual offence cases involving child witnesses under 13 years of age continues to operate in Belfast and Antrim Crown Courts and is delivering positive results. This protocol has seen a 70% reduction in the average time taken between reporting the crime and court disposal in cases falling within this voluntary protocol. The criminal justice organisations involved have also agreed plans to expedite, as much as possible, serious sexual offence cases involving child witnesses aged under 16 years in all Divisions. The development and roll-out of a Permanent Protocol will be subject to learning from both expedited processes and on additional funding becoming available; andthe ongoing im

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the effectiveness of legislation in protecting against sexual offenders (i) online; and (ii) offline.
answer_text: The Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 provides a comprehensive and robust framework to address a range of sexual offending behaviours irrespective of how the offence is carried out.  It is the main sexual offence framework in Northern Ireland but there are a number of others, depending on the offence committed. More recently, the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 strengthened the offences contained in the 2008 Order. This includes four new voyeurism offences to address behaviour commonly known as up-skirting and down-blousing, a new offence of sending an unwanted sexual image (commonly referred to as cyber-flashing) and four new grooming offences of an adult pretending to be a child which act as a precursor to the current grooming offences relating to children under 16. The Act also extended the offence of disclosing private sexual images to include threats to disclose, and the abuse of position of trust offences to bring the areas of religion and sport within its definition. The Act also removed the defence of “consent to serious harm” for sexual gratification.There are also a range of legislative measures used to manage risk and protect the public from sexual harm from sex offenders in the community. Under the Sexual Offences Act 2003 (‘the 2003 Act’), offenders convicted of certain sex offences are automatically subject to notification requirements under which they must provide personal information to the police – this is commonly known as ‘signing the sex offender register’. Breach of notification requirements is a criminal offence with a maximum penalty of five years imprisonment. Additional risk management tools, in the form of civil prevention orders, are used within the context of the Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland. These orders, also provided under the 2003 Act, enable the courts to apply tailored prohibitions or requirements on an offender’s behaviour where they are thought to pose a risk of sexual harm or serious sexual harm. Orders include the Sexual Offences Prevention Order, the Foreign Travel Order and the Risk of Sexual Harm Order.Breach of any of these civil prevention orders is a criminal offence with a maximum penalty of five years’ imprisonment. For the 2020/2021 cohort, those with a conviction where the principal offence was a sexual offence had the lowest overall reoffending rate of 9.1%, a pattern that was the same for adults and youths. Further detail on these statistics is available at Adult and Youth Reoffending in Northern Ireland (2020/21 Cohort)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when data access restrictions will be removed to enable renal patients across all Health and Social Care Trusts to view their medical results on Patient Knows Best.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any further plans to address energy inefficiency in households impacted by the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty intervention that will replace the current Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends in March 2026.  The intervention will continue to assist the most vulnerable low-income households by improving the energy efficiency of their homes, which will contribute to a reduction of energy consumption. The new scheme will align with the Climate Change Act, the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, and support the principles of the emerging Fuel Poverty Strategy which my Department is also working on. The new scheme will be part of the ’just transition’ to decarbonised home heating solutions going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of whether a reduction in corporation tax has the potential to attract investment into Northern Ireland and to boost the economy.
answer_text: The Independent Fiscal Commission NI in its Final Report of May 2022 observed that a lower corporation tax rate in the North could impact positively on investment decisions. It also noted that there would have to be agreement on how the block grant is adjusted, so that it would be affordable to the Executive in the short-term. This will be taken forward as part of negotiations on a new Fiscal Framework. It should also be noted that various factors influence investment decisions, including skills and access to markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions are being taken to address the ADHD waiting list in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there are currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department of Health is aware that this has led to variance in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of child and adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer for assessment and post-diagnostic support than is described in NICE guidelines.The Department of Health are committed to working closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced and evidenced-based and NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) do not provide an adult ADHD service and therefore do not hold a waiting list. SHSCT receive approximately 40 referrals per month for paediatric ADHD assessment and have capacity for 30 per month. There are currently 610 people under the age of 18, on the SHSCT waiting list with the longest wait of 440 days. The Department of Health is committed to meeting the needs of children and adults with ADHD. Officials are considering how a future ADHD service might be delivered, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis and post-diagnostic support has risen in recent years. As with all service developments, ADHD services will have to be considered in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to secure the provision of out-of-hours doctors in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) is commissioned to provide the GP Out of Hours (OOHs) service across the Southern area.  The GP OOHs service is for people who need urgent medical treatment but cannot wait until their doctor's practice reopens. The local population is encouraged to utilise the service appropriately to meet any urgent health care needs which cannot wait to be seen by their registered practice when it reopens.  The GP OOHs service is available Monday -Friday from 6pm to 8am the next morning, as well as 24 hours at weekends and during public holidays.  All patients can contact GP OOHs service via a single telephone number, there are no telephone numbers available to contact bases directly. The service’s bases are in Craigavon and Newry.  The majority of patients who contact SHSCT OOHs service do not need to be seen face-to-face.  From Apr 23-Jan 24, 82% of patient contacts were given advice by telephone or video call, 16% of patient contacts required a base visit and 2% of contacts required a home visit.  SHSCT OOHs are seeking to improve the skill mix through recruitment of ANPs, clinical pharmacist and nurse prescriber.  The GP OOH service provided by SHSCT has faced considerable challenges in consistently populating the GP rota.  Reasons for challenges include the fact that GPs are not contractually obliged to work in GP OOH; shifting attitudes in relation to achieving work life balance and preparedness or willingness of doctors to cover out of hours’ sessions; and busier and more complex day time general practice reducing the inclination or ability of GPs to work additional out of hours sessions.  When GPs book to work in out of hours clinics, the majority prefer to work within their local area - close to their own practices and homes.  The provision of  doctors contributing to the SHSCT OOHs has increased over the last year as figures demonstrate that GP Hours from Jan 22 – Jan 23 was 17,596 and from Jan 23 – Jan 24 was 23,748.The majority of clinical staff are retained by SHSCT OOHs on an ‘as and when required’ basis and for the reasons outlined above DoH does encourage the development of a multidisciplinary workforce.  Provision of a multidisciplinary workforce allows SHSCT OOHs to meet patient needs and make effective use of the available clinical resource.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment on the uptake of cardiopulmonary resuscitation training for Key Stage 3 pupils.
answer_text: Following an amendment to the Learning for Life and Work Area of Learning in the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007, post-primary schools have been required to deliver training in cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) to pupils in key stage 3 as a compulsory element of the curriculum since March 2022.  The Department does not, however, hold school level data on the number of pupils who have completed the training.The Department has and continues to partner with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment and the Education Authority to create the Community of Lifesavers Education Programme to support schools in meeting this requirement. As part of the Community of Lifesavers Education Programme, two teachers from every post primary, special and education other than at school (EOTAS) setting have been invited to attend training which focuses on the practical and essential CPR and automated external defibrillators (AED) lifesaving skills. The training was and continues to be facilitated by professionals from NIAS and helps teachers to plan for, and deliver, CPR training and AED awareness lessons in their school or setting.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the progress of the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s five year fleet replacement programme which started in January 2021.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service HSC Trust has made significant progress on the current five-year replacement programme.  This has been against the difficult circumstances encountered during the Pandemic and subsequently in relation to global supply issues. Dedicated funding to support fleet replacement has been provided to the Trust each year and this has allowed the replacement of vehicles in line with the planned programme.  The financial support has enabled NIAS to invest in a modern and reliable fleet utilising the latest technologies and efficiencies, particularly in relation to fuel efficiency and sustainability.  Vehicles purchased in the current 2023-24 financial year are currently being delivered and commissioned into service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people have been diagnosed with (i) Type 1 diabetes; and (ii) Type 2 diabetes, in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Diabetes is one of the disease registers in the Quality and Outcomes Framework.  Disease prevalence data collected as part of the QOF can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/prevalence-statistics.  The Diabetes register contains the number of patients aged 17 years and over with Diabetes Mellitus (specified as Type 1 or Type 2).  Although GP practices must record whether a patient has Type 1 or Type 2 Diabetes, this level of detail is not collected centrally, therefore the register size cannot be disaggregated by type of Diabetes.  QOF data is not broken down by Health and Social Care Trust but can be broken down by Local Commissioning Group (LCG); this data is shown in Table 1 below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the undeveloped land owned by the Housing Executive in the Upper Bann constituency.
answer_text: The undeveloped land owned by the Housing Executive in the Upper Bann constituency is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for a non-urgent appointment with an NHS dentist in the Upper Bann constituency.
answer_text: This information is not recorded by NI General Dental Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on capital works planned for GP practices in the Upper Bann constituency.
answer_text: A number of projects have recently been completed, are under way or are planned in the Upper Bann constituency to create additional capacity within GP practices.  These include:Banbridge Surgery and Scarva Street Surgery, Loughbrickland - capital works recently completed.Portadown Health Centre – capital works underway and due to complete late Spring 2024.Brownlow Health Centre and Gilford Health Centre – planning work is progressing, and delivery is subject to final business case approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department is taking to ensure farmers are able to access financial support for flood recovery.
answer_text: A business case was completed by DAERA to examine and rigorously test the need and value for money of a financial support scheme for potato growers impacted by flooding in counties Armagh and Down.  The conclusion was that government intervention is unlikely to confer a net benefit to the NI economy that would justify expenditure.  For this reason, DAERA has not implemented a support scheme. I am content to engage with stakeholders to explore arguments that might support a case for financial support to affected farm businesses, but any options must be cognisant of the rules governing the use of public money.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many roads require (i) resurfacing; and (ii) partial resurfacing, in Upper Bann.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area was presented to Council members in October 2023 and can be found at Annual Report to Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council 2023 (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk). The resurfacing programme along with other programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the level of care and support provided by diabetes healthcare teams since January 2022.
answer_text: Diabetes Services have been recovering post-Covid with additional investment to reduce waiting lists. Care delivery has been offered from diabetes specialist multidisciplinary teams with patients in higher risk groups prioritised for review first. However, waiting lists remain long with workforce challenges.An integrated response is required for an increasing population presenting with multi-morbidities, utilising all existing assets across workforce, infrastructure and with Community and Voluntary Sector partners.Working with key stakeholders, a Regional Integrated Pathway for Diabetes has been developed.  This Regional Pathway will standardise access and provide equity through integration of diabetes services, supporting treatment and care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what access to mental health and wellbeing support is available to those living with Type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: People living with Type 1 Diabetes (PLWD) can be referred to Psychology teams within local Trusts, as needed, for dedicated support.Local Trust Diabetes services use questionnaires with PLWD to identify areas of concern / for further discussion / referral to other services.Ongoing mental health and emotional wellbeing support is also provided by other professionals. This can include multidisciplinary diabetes specialist teams and mental health services. Support is provided during patient consultations, within diabetes education programmes and signposting to peer support networks. The Regional Insulin Pump service provides psychological support across the pathway with Quality of Life as a key indicator in pump decision making. NI is the only region to do this in the UK. There is recognition of ongoing workforce challenges and limited staff capacity to support these services further, as we would wish.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department has taken to enable greater access to new diabetes technologies to help management of blood glucose.
answer_text: The Department retains a formal link with the National Institute for health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC).On 14 February 2024, the Department endorsed National Institute for health and Care Excellence (NICE) Technical Appraisal (TA) 943 guidance https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta943 which recommends hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels as an option for the following people who are living with Type 1 diabetes: adults, where other conditions, as specified in TA 943, apply; children and young people (under 18 years old); andthose who are pregnant or planning a pregnancy. Full implementation of this guidance will require consideration of financial and service requirements including clinical capacity, specialist support and procurement routes. The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has been asked to work with Health and Social Care providers to develop an implementation plan which outlines a timescale for rollout that provides equitable, sustainable, and affordable access to this technology and which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the schools in Upper Bann that are awaiting the beginning or completion of a new build project; and (ii) to provide the current ranking for the prioritisation of works for those new school builds that are yet to begin.
answer_text: Eight schools in the Upper Bann constituency have been announced  under the Major Capital Works and Fresh Start Agreement Programmes. The status of these projects is outlined below.Major Capital Works Programme St Ronan’s College new school build project started on site in April 2023.  Construction is expected to complete in September 2025. Lismore Comprehensive’s new school building was completed in August 2023.  Demolition of the former school and external site works are estimated to complete in the coming weeks. Portadown Integrated Primary School new build project is currently in the design phase and a planning application for the project was submitted to Armagh City Banbridge & Craigavon Borough Council in late 2023.Kingspark Primary School & Nursery Unit new build project is in the early stages of development.  A technical feasibility study which explored options to deliver replacement accommodation has been completed and the Education Authority are currently drafting a Business Case which will identify the preferred option to be taken forward.	St Mary’s Primary School project is in the early stages of development.  A technical feasibility study has been completed and a Business Case has been drafted and is currently under consideration by the Department.Killicomaine Junior High School (JHS) was one of twenty-eight schools announced to advance in planning in March 2022. All twenty-eight projects were subsequently paused in April 2023, due to the capital budget position. On 12 February I advised that seven of the projects from the March 2022 announcement would now progress in planning, however Killicomaine JHS was not one of the seven projects, and thus this project remains paused.  Portadown College was another school which featured in the March 2022 announcement, with the project subsequently paused in April 2023.  Portadown College was one of the seven projects that I advised on 12 February would now progress in planning.  The procurement of an Integrated Consultant Team to undertake initial technical feasibility studies will now be progressed. 	Fresh Start AgreementNewbridge Integrated College new school build project commenced on site in January 2023, and is expected to complete in June 2025.	(ii)	Without additional capital funding projects that are in the design stages will not progress to construction and the twenty-one remaining projects from the March 2022 announcement will remain paused. Should capital funding become available projects will be reviewed and prioritized for delivery.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what measures the Executive Office has put in place to ensure decisions are taken in an open and transparent manner.
To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of (i) the current provision of special educational needs placements within Upper Bann; and (ii) whether he plans to increase this provision.
answer_text: There are significant pressures in the Upper Bann area in relation to specialist education provision which is currently provided in Ceara and Donard (special) Schools and specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS) classes in 11 primary and 5 post-primary schools.  Additionally, 2 primary schools in the area also operate classes for pupils with special school profiles.The Education Authority (EA) is working at pace to confirm demand for specialist education provision, to establish more places in special schools and to create more SPiMS for the 2024/25 academic year.  Schools in the Upper Bann area will be contacted over the next few weeks to discuss the possibility of establishing additional SPiMS classes for September 2024.   The EA has also recently acquired land adjacent to Donard School which will enable expansion of it in the medium to long-term to meet the increased demand for places.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans his Department has to support working families in making childcare more affordable.
answer_text: I outlined my plans to the Assembly on 12th February for the development and implementation of an ambitious Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  However, I am conscious that it will take time to get the right model in place and I am also considering what short-term measures might be possible to address current pressures.  Over the coming weeks, I plan to meet parents, providers, and others with an interest in the strategy, to hear firsthand the issues which they are facing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what measures his Department is taking to address rising Bovine Tuberculosis rates.
answer_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what measures his Department is taking to address rising Bovine Tuberculosis rates.Bovine Tuberculosis is one of the most challenging issues faced by the cattle farmers in Northern Ireland. I share Members’ concerns regarding the current high rate of TB incidence and the impact this is having on hard working farm families.  With herd incidence currently at over 10%, one farm in ten has experienced the difficulties associated with a new TB breakdown in the past year - and I have already heard directly from industry the devastating impact this is having.Tackling this disease is also having a significant impact on my Department’s budget.  53 million pounds was spent on the control of TB in the last financial year and a similar amount is expected this year.  The high cost of controlling TB is not sustainable- not to farm businesses nor to my Departmental budget.  I can assure the house that tackling this disease and all the factors which contribute to its spread and endurance will be a top priority for me in my new role.  I will be working closely with my officials in the coming weeks and engaging with stakeholders to examine the best way to help lower the rate of TB in our herds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the social housing waiting list in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area, broken down by (i) age; (ii) sex; and (iii) District Electoral Area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much his Department has spent on youth services in Upper Bann in the last 5 years.

An overview of funding that has been spent in Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Council Area includes those centres in the Upper Bann constituency. Due to the volume this information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans his Department has to enhance the place of schools' ethos in education.
answer_text: The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007, sets out statutory requirements relating to the curriculum through high level Areas of Learning. Specifically, it provides that the statutory curriculum: is balanced and broadly based which promotes the spiritual, emotional, moral, cultural, intellectual and physical development of pupils at the school and thereby of society; and prepares such pupils for the opportunities, responsibilities and experiences of life by equipping them with appropriate knowledge, understanding and skills.The statutory curriculum for Personal Development and Mutual Understanding at primary level and the Personal Development strand of Learning for Life and Work at post-primary level, prescribesthe high level minimum content for Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) at each key stage; this is the minimum entitlement that all young people must receive. The minimum content provides flexibility and schools and have a high degree of autonomy over what is taught.Relationships and Sexuality Education in the Northern Ireland Curriculum is not new; there were requirements for schools to deliver RSE before the introduction of the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (NI) 2007. For example, my Department issued guidance in 2001 (DE Circular 2001/15) accompanied by support materials produced by CCEA – advice for schools on developing or reviewing an RSE policy. In 2013, Circular 2013/16 required schools to have an RSE policy. In 2011, the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) published a report on RSE in post-primary schools; the Department of Education subsequentlywrote to schools in 2014 and published a revised guidance Circular in 2015 (Circular 2015/22) requiring schools to have an RSE policy based on their ethos and subject to consultation with parents and pupils and highlighting issues around dealing with sensitive issues. More recently in April 2023, the ETI published a thematic evaluation on the preventative curriculum (of which RSE is a part). My Department has established a task and finish group that is considering currently how best to respond to the ETI findings.On 5 June 2023 the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland brought forward Regulations under his powers in section 9 of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019 to ensure that the recommendations in paragraphs 85 and 86 of the 2018 report of the UN Committee on the Ending of Discrimination Against Women (“the CEDAW Report”) were implemented in respect of Northern Ireland. Paragraphs 85 and 86 recommended a change to the law around sexual and reproductive health, rights and services. Specific to education, paragraph 86(d) required the United Kingdom Government to “Make age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights a compulsory curriculum component for adolescents, covering early pregnancy prevention and access to abortion, and monitor its im

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to update hospital visitation guidance to allow more than two nominated individuals to visit each patient.
answer_text: Since the start of this summer, our hospitals/hospices have progressed to the final stage of the visiting Pathway, which allows that in general hospital settings: “One daily visit from up to two nominated individuals can be permitted at the same time, with particular effort made to keep the environment COVID secure. If the two nominated individuals are not from the same household and social distancing cannot be maintained, such visits can take place separately.  Duration of visits should be agreed with person in charge.  Alternatives, for example, outdoor visiting, virtual visits, should continue to be made available.”This is the minimum expected visiting arrangement, and individual wards/units are free to relax arrangements further if this is supported by an appropriate risk assessment.  My officials are working with PHA colleagues to develop new hospital/hospice visiting guidance which I anticipate will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are plans for further support for listed buildings through the Historic Environment Fund in 2023.
answer_text: Applications for support in the financial year 2022/23 have now closed. 126 applications were received to support repairs to listed buildings.Due to the continued absence of a functioning Executive and an agreed Executive Budget, my Department is operating within a constrained Contingency Planning Envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his Department's negotiations with Treasury on how the £400 Energy Bill Support Scheme will be administered in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I met with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on the 15th and 22nd June and discussed the need to provide a resolution to this issue.My department has subsequently had regular meetings with Treasury, the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy, the Department for the Economy and the Utility Regulator on how an equivalent Energy Bill Support Scheme might be introduced here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline whether there are any grants or assistance available for businesses to install charging points for electric vehicles.
answer_text: To support the introduction of electric vehicles the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) provides a range of EV related grants including grants for workplace charge points. The OZEV website can be viewed, via the following links, where details of funding to support the early market for ultra-low emission vehicles (ULEVs) can be found:https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/office-for-zero-emission-vehicleshttps://www.gov.uk/government/collections/government-grants-for-low-emission-vehicles

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline which reforms are planned to improve the Pre-Development Enquiry system in NI Water.
answer_text: Pursuant to my response to AQW1308/22-27, NI Water has been proactive in addressing its performance in respect of response times to customers regarding the impact assessment process.  Specifically NIW has: recruited additional temporary staff in late 2021, appointed two civil/environmental engineering consultancies to provide impact assessments, in addition to NI Water’s in-house team, modified the impact assessment process, to include an initial response to customers within 10 working days. is developing a bespoke single property impact assessment to better align with the scope and scale of this type of development.These improvements are as a result of feedback from NI Water’s customers and are intended to seek short to medium term solutions to allow development to continue within the current constrained networks.  In addition, my Department continues to work closely with NI Water, to identify if further improvements can be made to pre-development enquiry system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the Pre-Development Enquiry system within NI Water.
answer_text: NI Water introduced a new three stage pre planning process in April 2021, which included a modified pre-development enquiry (PDE), a requirement for impact assessment reports, and a planning response in their role as a statutory consultee. NI Water has advised that response times to customers in 2021/22 for both PDEs (811 received, with 87% meeting a target of 28 working days for a response) and planning responses (5847 received, with 94.9% meeting a target of 15 working days for a response) remains excellent.Performance metrics for the newly introduced Impact Assessment stage of the pre planning process are lower (364 received with a compliance of 19% meeting a service target of 65 working days for a response) however, NI Water is proactively working to significantly improve the performance of their process and expects to see significant improvement over the next quarter of 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many requests have NI Water received to adopt water treatment plants on private or social housing developments, broken down by constituency, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this data.  The adoption of water treatment plants on private or social housing developments is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should, therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many blood donation events took place in Upper Bann over the last twelve months.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service (NIBTS) have been in the Upper Bann area 15 times in the last 12 months and collected on a total of 30 days. Another donation session is scheduled for 23 and 24 June 2022 in Banbridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many (i) pre-school; (ii) primary; and (iii) post-primary pupils are currently without a confirmed school place for the incoming academic year in Upper Bann.
answer_text: The Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) has a two-stage admissions process which is based on parental preference.  Stages 1 and 2 of the process ended on 28 April 2022 and 16 June 2022 respectively. Following the close of Stage 2, the Education Authority (EA) has advised that of those children whose parents applied for a funded pre-school place within the Upper Bann constituency, three target aged children whose parents have stayed with the process to the end have not yet been offered a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.However, the EA has also advised that sufficient funded places remain available in the Upper Bann area to provide a funded place for every child in their immediate pre-school year whose parents want it and children will continue to be offered funded places prior to the beginning of the forthcoming pre-school year.The EA has also confirmed that no pupil who resides in Upper Bann and who applied for primary or Year 8 post-primary places through the respective admissions processes remain to secure a place.  Three children in the constituency are still to secure places at senior schools in the Dickson Plan (14+).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure which streets in Upper Bann are scheduled for large patchwork resurfacing work.
answer_text: Details of works programmes, including small scale resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be published within the Department’s Council Report for Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council area which is now being finalised following recent confirmation of my Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. In due course the Report will be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.I can also advise that the resurfacing contract for the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council area expired in November 2021 following legal challenges to the award of replacement resurfacing contracts. My Department has been working at pace to develop and implement a new term contract strategy to ensure that much needed resurfacing work is carried out in those areas without a contract, including within the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon area.My officials are currently aiming to have a replacement contract available towards the latter part of 2022. Subject to availability of funding, the majority of schemes that are currently on hold due to these contractual issues will be carried out as quickly as possible after the new contract has been awarded.The continued boycott of the Executive by the DUP means over £420m of funds held at the centre cannot be released. The blocking of the Executive also means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan for future investment in our roads network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many dentists there are in Upper Bann; and (ii) how many are offering NHS dental care.
answer_text: The number of dentists who have made arrangements with the former Health and Social Care Board to provide health service dentistry in the Upper Bann Assembly Area is shown in Table 1.  The figure is at 31st December 2021 and represents the latest publicly available information.
